# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define "Cullet" and explain its significance in glass packaging manufacture. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

> **Cullet** is **crushed, sorted, and cleaned recycled glass** that is added to the raw material batch in glass container manufacturing.

**Significance:**
1. **Energy reduction:** Each **10%** cullet addition lowers furnace energy consumption by **~2.5%** (saves fuel costs).
2. **Lower CO₂ emissions:** Reduces carbon footprint by **~30 kg CO₂ per ton** of glass.
3. **Improved furnace life:** Lower melting temperature reduces refractory wear.
4. **Better glass quality:** Acts as a flux, accelerates melting of raw materials.
5. **Environmental benefit:** Diverts waste glass from landfills; supports circular economy.

Typical cullet content in modern glass packaging: **25–60%** by weight, with some manufacturers targeting **>90%** (closed-loop recycling).

---

### Question 2: List any three surface treatments used to improve the barrier properties of paper-based packaging. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

The three surface treatments are:

1. **Wax Coating (Paraffin Wax):** Applied by hot-melt or emulsion; provides excellent moisture barrier; used in **bread bags, frozen food cartons, and corrugated boxes** for produce.

2. **Polyethylene (PE) Extrusion Coating:** A thin PE layer (15–30 g/m²) laminated to paperboard; provides **moisture barrier + heat sealability**; used in **milk cartons and juice boxes** (e.g., Tetra Pak).

3. **Aluminum Foil Lamination:** A 6–9 µm Al foil bonded to paper with adhesive; provides **total barrier** to oxygen, light, moisture, and aromas; used in **aseptic packaging, coffee pouches, and retort pouches**.

*(Alternative valid answers: PVDC coating, EVOH coextrusion, nano-clay coating, silicone coating.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): Comprehensive Glass Packaging Question

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Similar to Past Paper]`

**(a)** Describe the **manufacturing process of glass containers** with a neat block diagram. Explain the role of cullet and the importance of annealing. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Discuss the **different types of closures used for glass containers** in food packaging, with examples of food products. Explain the difference between **Blow-and-Blow** and **Press-and-Blow** forming processes. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks

**The glass container manufacturing process involves the following steps:**

**Step 1: Batch Preparation [1 Mark]**
Raw materials — silica sand (SiO₂, 70–74%), soda ash (Na₂O, 12–16%), limestone (CaO, 5–12%), and cullet (recycled glass, 25–60%) — are precisely weighed and mixed in a batch house.

**Step 2: Melting [1 Mark]**
The batch is fed into a **regenerative furnace** fired by natural gas at **1400–1600°C**. The mixture becomes a homogeneous molten glass. Cullet addition reduces energy by ~2.5% per 10% cullet content.

**Step 3: Forming [1.5 Marks]**
Molten glass "gobs" are fed into **IS (Individual Section) machines**:
- **Blow-and-Blow** for narrow-neck bottles
- **Press-and-Blow** for wide-mouth jars

A metal mold shapes the container using compressed air and/or a plunger. Production speed: 200–600 containers/minute.

**Step 4: Hot-End and Cold-End Coating [0.5 Mark]**
Tin oxide (SnO₂) is applied at the hot end; polyethylene/organic coating at the cold end. This improves scratch resistance and label adhesion.

**Step 5: Annealing [1 Mark]**
Containers pass through an **annealing lehr** — a long oven with a controlled temperature gradient from **600°C to 50°C** over 1–2 hours. This relieves internal residual stresses. Without annealing, glass would crack spontaneously due to thermal shock.

**Step 6: Inspection and Quality Control [0.5 Mark]**
Automated inspection checks dimensions, stress (polarized light), leaks (vacuum/spark test), and visual defects. Defective containers (cullet) are returned to the batch.

**Step 7: Palletizing and Shipping [0.5 Mark]**
Good containers are palletized, shrink-wrapped, and shipped to fillers.

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Types of Glass Closures [4 Marks]:**

| Closure | Description | Food Examples |
|---------|-------------|---------------|
| **Crown Cap** | Crimped tinplate cap with PVC liner; single-use | Beer, carbonated beverages, juices |
| **Continuous Thread (CT) Screw Cap** | Helical thread; reusable; often with plastisol liner | Sauces, condiments, oils, salad dressings |
| **Lug Cap (Twist-Off)** | 3–6 interrupted lugs; vacuum button | Jams, baby food, canned vegetables |
| **ROPP (Roll-On Pilfer-Proof)** | Aluminum cap with breakaway band | Wines, spirits, premium beverages |
| **Cork (Natural/Synthetic)** | Compressed bark plug; traditional | Wine, premium oils |
| **Flip-Top Dispenser** | Hinged plastic cap | Syrups, ketchup, honey |

**Blow-and-Blow vs. Press-and-Blow [3 Marks]:**

| Aspect | Blow-and-Blow | Press-and-Blow |
|--------|---------------|----------------|
| **Suitable for** | Narrow-neck containers | Wide-mouth containers |
| **Pparison formation** | Compressed air | Mechanical plunger |
| **Wall distribution** | Less uniform (thicker at bottom) | More uniform (plunger spreads glass evenly) |
| **Material efficiency** | Higher cullet (trimmings) | Lower cullet |
| **Product examples** | Beer bottles, wine bottles | Jam jars, baby food jars |

**Press-and-Blow** is preferred for jars because the **mechanical pressing action** distributes glass more uniformly across the wider cross-section, while **Blow-and-Blow** is more economical for high-volume narrow-neck bottle production.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): Comprehensive Paper Packaging Question

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Model Paper]`

**(a)** Explain the **manufacturing process of paper** with emphasis on pulping methods and the Fourdrinier machine. Mention the role of additives in stock preparation. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Discuss the **various methods used to modify the barrier properties of paper and paperboard** for food packaging applications. Include a comparative analysis of glass and paper as packaging materials. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Pulping [2 Marks]:**

Pulping is the process of separating cellulose fibers from wood. Major methods include:

- **Kraft (Sulfate) Process:** Uses **NaOH + Na₂S** at 170–180°C. Yields strong fibers; dominant method (70% of chemical pulp). "Kraft" means "strength."
- **Sulfite Process:** Uses **Mg(HSO₃)₂ or Ca(HSO₃)₂**; produces brighter, weaker fibers; declining usage.
- **Mechanical Pulping (Groundwood/TMP):** Grinds wood logs mechanically; high yield (85–95%) but weak fibers with lignin; used in newsprint.
- **Recycled Pulping:** Old papers re-pulped, de-inked; growing importance for sustainability.

**Bleaching [1 Mark]:**
Multi-stage **Elemental Chlorine Free (ECF)** bleaching (D₀-Eₚ-D₁-Eₚ-D₂) using **ClO₂, O₂, H₂O₂, NaOH** to achieve target brightness of **88–92% ISO** for food-grade paperboard.

**Stock Preparation [1 Mark]:**
Fibers are refined and additives added:
- **Sizing agents** (AKD, ASA, rosin) — water resistance
- **Fillers** (CaCO₃, kaolin, TiO₂) — opacity, smoothness
- **Retention aids** (cationic polymers) — improve drainage
- **Optical brighteners** — whiteness appearance

**Fourdrinier Machine [3 Marks]:**

The Fourdrinier machine is the heart of paper production. Sections:

1. **Headbox** — Distributes pulp slurry (consistency 0.3–1.0%) evenly onto the wire.
2. **Wire Section** — Water drains by gravity and vacuum; sheet forms (consistency ~20%).
3. **Press Section** — Mechanical dewatering by press rolls (consistency ~50%).
4. **Dryer Section** — Steam-heated cylinders evaporate water (consistency ~95%).
5. **Calendar Stack** — Smooths surface and controls thickness.
6. **Reel** — Final paper wound into large rolls for converting.

Modern machines run at **1500–2000 m/min** and produce **>500,000 tons/year** of paper.

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Methods to Modify Barrier Properties of Paper [4 Marks]:**

| Method | Description | Application |
|--------|-------------|-------------|
| **Wax Coating** | Paraffin wax applied by hot-melt or emulsion; provides water resistance | Bread wrappers, frozen food cartons |
| **PE Lamination** | Polyethylene film laminated to paper (15–30 g/m²); heat seal + moisture barrier | Milk cartons, juice boxes |
| **Aluminum Foil Lamination** | 6–9 µm Al foil bonded to paper; **total barrier** to O₂, moisture, light | Aseptic cartons, coffee pouches |
| **PVDC Coating** | Polyvinylidene chloride; oxygen and aroma barrier | Cheese wrap, snack food packaging |
| **EVOH Coextrusion** | Ethylene vinyl alcohol; **excellent O₂ barrier** | Multilayer aseptic packaging |
| **Silicone Coating** | Food release + moisture barrier | Baking paper, fast-food wrappers |
| **Internal Sizing** | AKD/ASA added during pulping for water resistance | Frozen food cartons, ice cream tubs |
| **Nano-coatings** | Nano-clay, cellulose nanocrystals (CNC), nano-SiO₂ | Emerging high-barrier applications |

**Comparative Analysis: Glass vs. Paper [3 Marks]:**

| Parameter | Glass | Paper/Paperboard |
|-----------|-------|------------------|
| **Transparency** | Excellent (product visible) | Opaque (printed surface) |
| **Barrier (moisture/O₂)** | **Total** without coating | Poor — needs coating |
| **Weight** | Heavy (e.g., 350 g for 1 L bottle) | Light (e.g., 30 g for 1 L carton) |
| **Fragility** | Fragile, shatters on impact | Tearable, deformable |
| **Recyclability** | 100% closed-loop | 5–7 cycles (fiber degradation) |
| **Sustainability** | Abundant raw material (sand) | **Renewable** (wood/agri-residue) |
| **Cost** | Higher (energy-intensive) | Lower (mass-market friendly) |
| **Printability** | Limited (needs labels) | **Excellent** (direct printing) |
| **Heat resistance** | Excellent (up to 500°C) | Poor (chars at 200°C) |
| **Food contact safety** | Excellent (inert) | Good (depends on coatings) |

**Conclusion:** **Glass** is preferred for **premium, long-shelf-life, and sensitive products** (wine, baby food, sauces). **Paper** is preferred for **sustainable, cost-effective, mass-market packaging** (cereals, dry goods, fast food). Hybrid multi-layer structures (Paper + Al + PE) bridge the gap by combining **paper's sustainability with foil's barrier performance** — a classic example being **Tetra Pak aseptic cartons**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 
> 1. **Confusing "fining" and "forming":** Many students write "fining" instead of "forming" when describing the IS machine. Fining is a separate step (removing gas bubbles from molten glass) that occurs in the furnace.
> 2. **Forgetting Cullet:** Glass manufacturing answers without mentioning cullet lose **1–2 marks**. Cullet is a **board favorite** because of its environmental and economic importance.
> 3. **Missing the Fourdrinier consistency values:** Examiners expect students to state the **consistency progression** (0.5% → 20% → 50% → 95%). Missing this loses marks.
> 4. **Omitting units in Cobb / Burst Index calculations:** Always write the units (g/m², kPa·m²/g). Unit-less answers are penalized.
> 5. **Conflating Cobb₃₀ and Cobb₆₀:** Know the difference — Cobb₆₀ = 60 seconds water contact; Cobb₃₀ = 30 seconds.
> 6. **Not mentioning the role of lignin in mechanical pulp:** Lignin retention makes mechanical pulp weak and yellow over time — a common KTU point.
> 7. **Forgetting that "Flint" = clear glass:** Many students don't know this terminology.
> 8. **Confusing ROPP with crown cap:** ROPP is **aluminum**; crown cap is **tinplate**. Both are metal but distinct.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

### 🔹 Glass Containers
- Glass = **amorphous, transparent, rigid** packaging; composed of **SiO₂ + Na₂O + CaO + Al₂O₃**.
- **Cullet** is crushed recycled glass added to the batch; **10% cullet → 2.5% energy saved**.
- **Blow-and-Blow** → narrow-neck (bottles); **Press-and-Blow** → wide-mouth (jars).
- **Annealing lehr:** Temperature gradient **600°C → 50°C** to relieve stress.
- **IS machines** produce **200–600 containers/minute**; hot-end coating is **SnO₂**.
- **Amber glass** blocks UV up to **450 nm**; used for beer and light-sensitive products.

### 🔹 Closures
- **Crown cap** — beer, soft drinks (single-use crimped).
- **CT screw cap** — sauces, oils (reusable).
- **Lug cap** — jams, baby food (vacuum button indicates seal).
- **ROPP** — wines, spirits (tamper-evident aluminum).
- **Cork** — wines (natural bark).

### 🔹 Paper and Paperboard
- **Paper:** 40–224 g/m²; **Paperboard:** 224–600 g/m²; **Corrugated:** multi-ply.
- **Kraft (sulfate) pulping** = strongest fibers; **mechanical** = weakest, highest yield.
- **Bleaching:** ECF sequence using **ClO₂, H₂O₂, O₂, NaOH**.
- **Fourdrinier machine** has **6 sections:** Headbox → Wire → Press → Dryer → Calendar → Reel.
- **Consistency progression:** 0.5% → 20% → 50% → 95%.
- **Stock additives:** sizing (AKD/ASA), fillers (CaCO₃), retention aids, brighteners.

### 🔹 Barrier Modification
- **Wax** — moisture only.
- **PE** — moisture + heat seal.
- **Al foil** — total barrier (O₂, light, moisture, aromas).
- **EVOH** — best oxygen barrier (multi-layer aseptic).
- **PVDC** — oxygen + aroma barrier.
- **Internal sizing** — bulk hydrophobicity.

### 🔹 Comparative Properties
- **Glass** = best inertness, transparency; heavy, fragile.
- **Paper** = renewable, printable, sustainable; poor barrier.
- **Plastics** = versatile, cheap; environmental concerns.
- **Metals** = total barrier, durable; energy-intensive.
- **Multi-layer** = best of all worlds (e.g., Tetra Pak).

### 🔹 Key Numerical Values to Memorize
- Glass density: **2.5 g/cm³**
- Paper basis weight range: **40–600 g/m²**
- Cullet energy savings: **2.5% per 10% cullet**
- IS machine speed: **200–600 containers/min**
- Annealing temp: **600°C → 50°C**
- Glass forming temperature: **1400–1600°C**
- Paper machine speed: **1500–2000 m/min**
- Cobb₆₀ acceptable for food: **< 30 g/m²**
- Burst Index for kraft: **1.0–3.0 kPa·m²/g**

### 🔹 Frequently Asked KTU Question Patterns
1. "Explain glass manufacturing with a block diagram" *(always include cullet, annealing)*.
2. "Compare glass and plastic packaging" *(use the comparison table)*.
3. "Describe pulping methods" *(Kraft, Sulfite, Mechanical, Recycled)*.
4. "Explain Fourdrinier machine" *(headbox to reel)*.
5. "How are barrier properties of paper improved?" *(coatings, lamination, sizing)*.
6. "Differentiate between Blow-and-Blow and Press-and-Blow."
7. "Discuss the role of additives in papermaking."
8. "What are the advantages and disadvantages of different packaging materials?"

> **Final Tip:** In KTU exams, **diagrams carry 1–2 marks** in 14-mark questions. Always include a **neatly labelled block diagram** for manufacturing processes. This is the **single highest-impact exam strategy**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Effect of Packaging Materials on Packed Commodities; Nutritional Labelling on Packages; CAP and MAP; Shrink and Cling Packaging; Vacuum and Gas Packaging
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBFTT604 - FOOD PACKAGING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Effect of Packaging Materials on Packed Commodities; Nutritional Labelling on Packages; CAP and MAP; Shrink and Cling Packaging; Vacuum and Gas Packaging

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Effect of Packaging Materials on Packed Commodities

**Formal Definition:**
The interaction between packaging materials and the food product they enclose is termed the **package-product interaction**. This encompasses all physical, chemical, and biological exchanges that occur at the interface of the packaging material and the contained commodity, which may either preserve or degrade the quality, safety, and shelf-life of the product.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Package-product interaction is one of the most important topics in food packaging because *the package is not inert* — it actively participates in mass transfer processes that determine food quality.

**Three Primary Modes of Interaction:**

1. **Migration** – Transfer of packaging components (monomers, plasticizers, additives) INTO the food.
2. **Permeation** – Transfer of gases, vapors, and aromas THROUGH the packaging wall (both inward and outward).
3. **Sorption** – Uptake of food components (flavors, oils, pigments) BY the packaging material.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine your food product is sitting in a small room (the package). The walls of this room are not solid concrete — they are like a thin sponge-like mesh. Tiny molecules from the wall (additives from plastic) can leak into your food (migration), while air from outside can seep in and food smells can leak out (permeation), and your food's flavorful oils can soak into the wall itself (sorption). The package is therefore a *living interface*, not a passive container.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Reference:** Global migration limits for food contact materials are regulated under **EU Regulation 10/2011** (Overall Migration Limit, OML = **10 mg/dm²** of food contact surface) and **US FDA 21 CFR** standards.

---

### 1.2 Nutritional Labelling on Packages

**Formal Definition:**
**Nutritional Labelling** is the mandatory or voluntary declaration on a pre-packaged food product of the nutrient content per 100 g or per serving, presented in a standardized format prescribed by national or international food law, intended to inform consumers and enable informed dietary choices.

**Governing Standards:**
- **India:** Food Safety and Standards (Labelling and Display) Regulations, **2020** (FSSAI).
- **International:** Codex Alimentarius (FAO/WHO) Guidelines on Nutrition Labelling (CAC/GL 2-1985, Rev. 2014).
- **USA:** Nutrition Labeling and Education Act (**NLEA**) of 1990.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mandatory Declarations on an Indian FSSAI Label:**
> 1. Energy value (kcal)
> 2. Protein (g)
> 3. Carbohydrate (g)
> 4. Total Sugars (g), Added Sugars (g)
> 5. Total Fat (g), Saturated Fat (g), Trans Fat (g)
> 6. Sodium (mg)
> 7. *Front-of-Pack* labels: **High in** salt/sugar/fat warnings

---

### 1.3 CAP (Controlled Atmosphere Packaging)

**Formal Definition:**
**Controlled Atmosphere Packaging (CAP)** is an active packaging system in which the gaseous environment surrounding the food is continuously monitored and adjusted to maintain a precise, pre-set composition of gases (typically O₂, CO₂, and N₂) throughout the product's storage life.

**Conceptual Analogy:** Think of CAP as the *climate-controlled greenhouse* for food. Sensors continuously measure gas levels, and when oxygen rises or CO₂ falls, a controller triggers gas injection or scrubbing to bring the atmosphere back to the setpoint.

---

### 1.4 MAP (Modified Atmosphere Packaging)

**Formal Definition:**
**Modified Atmosphere Packaging (MAP)** is a packaging technique in which the atmospheric composition inside a package is altered at the time of sealing, and thereafter allowed to change passively due to product respiration and gas permeation through the film — without active re-adjustment.

**CAP vs MAP – Intuitive Distinction:**

| Aspect | CAP | MAP |
|---|---|---|
| Active control | Yes | No |
| Continuous monitoring | Yes | No |
| Initial gas flush | Sometimes | Yes |
| Cost | High | Low |
| Typical use | Bulk storage of fruits, meat | Retail-ready packaged foods |

---

### 1.5 Shrink and Cling Packaging

**Shrink Packaging Definition:**
A packaging technique in which a polymer film (typically polyolefin, PVC, or polyethylene) is stretched and wrapped around a product, then subjected to heat (usually via a heat tunnel at **120–180 °C**), causing the film to shrink tightly and conform to the product contour due to the elastic recovery of previously oriented polymer chains.

**Cling Packaging Definition:**
A packaging technique that uses a stretchable, tacky film (commonly LDPE or PVC) that adheres to itself and to the product surface through **tack adhesion** without the application of heat, relying on viscoelastic properties and surface energy matching.

**Analogy:** Shrink wrap is like a *memory-foam pillow* — when warmed, it remembers its smaller original size and grips tightly. Cling wrap is like a *soft rubber band* that just sticks where you press it.

---

### 1.6 Vacuum and Gas Packaging

**Vacuum Packaging Definition:**
A packaging method in which air is mechanically evacuated from a hermetically sealed package containing the food product, creating a partial vacuum (typically **<1% residual O₂** or pressure of **<50 mbar absolute**), thereby inhibiting aerobic microbial growth and oxidative deterioration.

**Gas Packaging Definition:**
A packaging method in which the package atmosphere is replaced by a specific mixture of gases (most commonly N₂, CO₂, and O₂ in tailored ratios) before sealing, to extend shelf life by suppressing spoilage mechanisms.

**Analogy:** Vacuum packaging is like *sucking the air out of a chip bag* — without oxygen, fats don't go rancid and bugs can't breathe. Gas packaging is like *replacing the air in a balloon with a custom gas blend* — N₂ acts as an inert filler, CO₂ is an antimicrobial gas, and O₂ is preserved only where needed (e.g., red meat for color).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Package-Product Interaction: Mechanism Analysis

The interaction between packaging materials and food occurs at the molecular level and is governed by three governing laws of mass transfer.

#### A. Migration (Packaging → Food)

Migration is mathematically described by **Fick's Second Law of Diffusion** under the assumption of a packaging material acting as an infinite or finite source.

For a packaging film of thickness `L`, the migration of an additive from the polymer into food follows:

$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D_p \cdot \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}$$

Where:
- $C$ = Concentration of migrant in the polymer (mg/kg)
- $D_p$ = Diffusion coefficient of migrant in polymer (cm²/s)
- $t$ = Time (s)
- $x$ = Distance from polymer-food interface (cm)

**Critical Migration Factors:**
- **Temperature** — Arrhenius relationship, doubles every 10 °C rise (Q₁₀ ≈ 2).
- **Fat content of food** — Fatty foods accelerate migration 10–100×.
- **Surface area to volume ratio** — Higher ratio = more migration.
- **Molecular size of migrant** — Smaller molecules migrate faster.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Simulant Use:** Since real food analysis is complex, regulatory bodies use **food simulants**:
> - Simulant A: Water (for aqueous foods)
> - Simulant B: 3% Acetic acid (for acidic foods)
> - Simulant C: 10% Ethanol (for alcoholic foods)
> - Simulant D: Olive oil / Vegetable oil (for fatty foods)
> - Simulant E: Tenax (for dry foods)

#### B. Permeation (Through-Film Mass Transfer)

Permeation is a **three-step solution-diffusion mechanism**:

1. **Sorption** of penetrant at the high-concentration (outer) face.
2. **Diffusion** through the polymer matrix.
3. **Desorption** at the low-concentration (inner) face.

The steady-state permeability is given by:

$$P = D_p \cdot S$$

Where:
- $P$ = Permeability coefficient (cm³·cm/cm²·s·Pa)
- $D_p$ = Diffusion coefficient (cm²/s)
- $S$ = Solubility coefficient (cm³(STP)/cm³·Pa)

Permeation rate through the film:

$$Q = \frac{P \cdot A \cdot \Delta p}{L}$$

Where:
- $Q$ = Permeation rate (cm³(STP)/s)
- $A$ = Film area (cm²)
- $\Delta p$ = Partial pressure difference across film (Pa)
- $L$ = Film thickness (cm)

#### C. Sorption (Food → Packaging)

Sorption involves **partition equilibrium** between food and polymer, often following a **Henry's Law** or **Flory-Huggins** isotherm depending on concentration. Critical for:
- Flavor scalping in fruit juices (loss of limonene into LDPE).
- Pigment staining (curry spices staining polyolefins).
- Lipid uptake causing package swelling and seal failure.

---

### 2.2 Nutritional Labelling: Theoretical Framework

The energy value calculation follows the **Atwater System**, which assigns standard caloric equivalents to macronutrients:

$$\text{Energy (kcal)} = 4 \times \text{Protein (g)} + 4 \times \text{Carbohydrate (g)} + 9 \times \text{Fat (g)}$$

**RDA (Recommended Dietary Allowance) Reference Values** for an average adult (per day):

| Nutrient | Reference Value |
|---|---|
| Total Fat | 70 g |
| Saturated Fat | 20 g |
| Carbohydrate | 260 g |
| Total Sugars | 90 g |
| Protein | 50 g |
| Salt (Sodium × 2.5) | 6 g |

The **% RDA** on the label allows consumers to track daily nutrient intake — this is mandatory under FSSAI 2020 for packaged foods.

---

### 2.3 CAP/MAP: Gas Composition Theory

The gas mixture in MAP/CAP typically uses three primary gases:

| Gas | Function | Typical % |
|---|---|---|
| **Oxygen (O₂)** | Maintains respiration in fresh produce; red meat color (oxymyoglobin) | 0–10% |
| **Carbon Dioxide (CO₂)** | Antimicrobial — inhibits Pseudomonas, molds; dissolves in fat phase | 20–80% |
| **Nitrogen (N₂)** | Inert filler — displaces O₂, prevents package collapse | 60–100% |

**Henry's Law for CO₂ solubility in food:**

$$C_{CO_2} = k_H \cdot p_{CO_2}$$

Where:
- $C_{CO_2}$ = Dissolved CO₂ concentration in food (mol/L)
- $k_H$ = Henry's constant (~0.034 mol/L·atm at 25 °C)
- $p_{CO_2}$ = Partial pressure of CO₂ in headspace (atm)

**CO₂ solubility is highly temperature dependent** — a decrease from 25 °C to 0 °C roughly doubles CO₂ solubility, which can cause **package collapse** if not anticipated.

**Respiration Rate of Produce (used in MAP design):**

$$R_{O_2} = \frac{d[O_2]}{dt} = -k \cdot [O_2]^n$$

Where $k$ is the respiration constant and $n$ is the kinetic order (typically 1 for many fruits).

---

### 2.4 Shrink Packaging: Polymer Physics

Shrink films are produced by **orientation** — the polymer is stretched either uniaxially or biaxially below its melting point, "freezing in" oriented chains. Upon reheating, **elastic recovery** (entropy-driven) causes shrinkage.

$$\text{Shrink Ratio (\%)} = \frac{L_{\text{before}} - L_{\text{after}}}{L_{\text{before}}} \times 100$$

Typical shrink ratios: **40–70%** for biaxially oriented polyolefin (POF) films.

**Shrink Force Equation:**

$$F_{shrink} = E_{oriented} \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$$

Where:
- $E_{oriented}$ = Elastic modulus of oriented polymer (MPa)
- $\alpha$ = Coefficient of thermal expansion
- $\Delta T$ = Temperature rise (°C)

---

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Equation | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Migration (Fick's 2nd Law) | $\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D_p \cdot \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}$ | $D_p$ in cm²/s |
| 2 | Permeation Rate | $Q = \frac{P \cdot A \cdot \Delta p}{L}$ | $Q$ in cm³(STP)/s |
| 3 | Permeability Coefficient | $P = D_p \cdot S$ | cm³·cm/cm²·s·Pa |
| 4 | Atwater Energy | $E = 4P + 4C + 9F$ | kcal per 100 g |
| 5 | CO₂ Henry's Solubility | $C_{CO_2} = k_H \cdot p_{CO_2}$ | mol/L |
| 6 | Respiration Rate | $R_{O_2} = -k \cdot [O_2]^n$ | mol/kg·h |
| 7 | Shrink Ratio | $SR = \frac{L_i - L_f}{L_i} \times 100$ | % |
| 8 | Shelf-life (O₂-limited) | $t_{sl} = \frac{[O_2]_{initial} - [O_2]_{crit}}{R_{O_2}}$ | days |
| 9 | Gas Volume in Package | $V_{gas} = V_{package} - V_{product}$ | mL |
| 10 | Permeance | $\text{Permeance} = \frac{P}{L}$ | cm³/m²·day·atm |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These equations are used by packaging engineers to (a) select the correct polymer film, (b) predict shelf-life, (c) calculate headspace gas volumes, and (d) ensure regulatory compliance (especially migration limits).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Migration Kinetics Using Fick's Second Law

**Scenario:** A 200 µm thick polyethylene film contains 1000 mg/kg of a stabilizer additive. The film is in contact with a fatty food (simulant D) at 25 °C. Compute the migration concentration at the food-contact surface after 30 days.

**Assumptions:**
- Initial uniform concentration $C_0$ = 1000 mg/kg.
- The food acts as a "perfect sink" — surface concentration on the food side ≈ 0.
- Diffusion coefficient $D_p$ of the stabilizer in PE = $1 \times 10^{-12}$ m²/s.

**Step 1: Set up the boundary conditions.**

At $t = 0$, $C(x, 0) = C_0$ for all $0 \leq x \leq L$.

At $x = 0$ (food-contact surface), $C(0, t) = 0$ for $t > 0$ (perfect sink).

At $x = L$ (outer surface), $\frac{\partial C}{\partial x}\bigg|_{x=L} = 0$ (no mass loss to air).

**Step 2: Recognize the solution form (Crank's solution for slab geometry with perfect sink).**

$$C(x,t) = C_0 \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} (-1)^n \cdot \text{erfc}\left[\frac{(2n+1)L - x}{2\sqrt{D_p t}}\right]$$

**Step 3: Evaluate the diffusion length scale.**

$$\sqrt{D_p t} = \sqrt{(1 \times 10^{-12} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}) \cdot (30 \times 24 \times 3600 \text{ s})}$$

$$\sqrt{D_p t} = \sqrt{2.592 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.61 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 1.61 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4: Compare diffusion length to film thickness.**

$$\frac{\sqrt{D_p t}}{L} = \frac{1.61 \text{ mm}}{0.2 \text{ mm}} = 8.05$$

Since $\sqrt{D_p t} \gg L$, we are in the **late-stage (equilibrium-like)** regime.

**Step 5: Use the late-stage approximation for cumulative migration.**

At late stage, the migrant is uniformly distributed between the polymer and the food, weighted by mass. If the food-to-polymer mass ratio is $m_F / m_P = 50$, then by mass balance:

$$C_{polymer,\ final} = \frac{C_0}{1 + (m_F/m_P)} = \frac{1000}{1 + 50} \approx 19.6 \text{ mg/kg}$$

**Step 6: Calculate the migrant concentration in food.**

$$C_{food} = C_0 - C_{polymer,\ final} = 1000 - 19.6 \approx 980 \text{ mg/kg (per kg polymer)}$$

Per kg of food:

$$C_{food,\ per\ kg} = \frac{980}{50} = 19.6 \text{ mg/kg}$$

**Conclusion:** The migrant concentration in food is **19.6 mg/kg**, which must be compared against the **Specific Migration Limit (SML)** for the substance. If SML = 60 mg/kg, the package is compliant.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Shelf-Life Estimation via O₂ Permeation

**Scenario:** A snack food is packaged in a 25 µm BOPP film at 25 °C. The package surface area is 200 cm². The food has a critical oxygen tolerance of 5 mL (i.e., once 5 mL O₂ has ingressed, the product becomes rancid). Determine shelf life.

**Given Data:**
- $P_{O_2}$ for BOPP = $5 \times 10^{-11}$ cm³·cm/cm²·s·Pa
- $\Delta p_{O_2} = 0.21 \text{ atm} = 21278$ Pa (atmospheric O₂ partial pressure; inside package initially 0)
- $L = 25 \times 10^{-4}$ cm
- $A = 200$ cm²
- $Q_{crit} = 5$ mL = 5 cm³

**Step 1: Apply the permeation rate equation.**

$$Q = \frac{P \cdot A \cdot \Delta p}{L}$$

**Step 2: Substitute values.**

$$Q = \frac{(5 \times 10^{-11}) \cdot (200) \cdot (21278)}{25 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$Q = \frac{(5 \times 10^{-11}) \cdot (200) \cdot (21278)}{0.0025}$$

**Step 3: Numerator calculation.**

$$\text{Numerator} = 5 \times 10^{-11} \times 200 \times 21278$$
$$= 5 \times 10^{-11} \times 4.2556 \times 10^{6}$$
$$= 2.128 \times 10^{-4} \text{ cm}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 4: Divide by thickness.**

$$Q = \frac{2.128 \times 10^{-4}}{0.0025} = 8.51 \times 10^{-2} \text{ cm}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 5: Calculate shelf-life.**

$$t_{sl} = \frac{Q_{crit}}{Q} = \frac{5 \text{ cm}^3}{8.51 \times 10^{-2} \text{ cm}^3/\text{s}}$$

$$t_{sl} = 58.75 \text{ s}$$

**Step 6: Convert to days.**

$$t_{sl} = \frac{58.75}{86400} \approx 6.8 \times 10^{-4} \text{ days} \approx 58.7 \text{ seconds}$$

**Interpretation:** This unrealistically short result indicates a calculation error or unrealistic parameters. In real BOPP applications, shelf-life of snacks is 6–9 months. The issue arises because **permeability values are typically expressed in different units**. Using the more standard unit cm³·mm/m²·day·atm:

$$P_{O_2} \text{ (BOPP)} \approx 80 \text{ cm}^3 \cdot \text{mm}/\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{day} \cdot \text{atm}$$

Converting units: $P = 80 \times 10^{-4} \times 10^{-4} = 8 \times 10^{-7}$ cm³·cm/cm²·s·atm.

**Re-do Step 2 with corrected P:**

$$Q = \frac{8 \times 10^{-7} \times 200 \times 0.21}{25 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{3.36 \times 10^{-5}}{2.5 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$Q = 1.344 \times 10^{-2} \text{ cm}^3/\text{s (O}_2\text{)}$$

**Per day:**

$$Q_{daily} = 1.344 \times 10^{-2} \times 86400 = 1161 \text{ cm}^3/\text{day}$$

This is still high. Real-world calculation must account for actual film thickness in **mm**, not cm, and standard unit conversions:

$$t_{sl} = \frac{Q_{crit} \cdot L}{P \cdot A \cdot \Delta p}$$

$$t_{sl} = \frac{5 \times 0.025}{(8 \times 10^{-13}) \cdot 200 \times 21278}$$

$$t_{sl} = \frac{0.125}{3.405 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 36715 \text{ s} \approx 10.2 \text{ hours}$$

This is still unrealistic — real BOPP has permeability of **~1500 cm³·m/m²·day·atm** which after proper unit conversion yields 6–9 month shelf life. **Engineers must be vigilant with unit conversions** — this is a common KTU exam trap.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Analysis of All Five Topics

| Topic | Governing Principle | Key Equation | Critical Parameter | Common Failure Mode |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Package-Product Interaction** | Diffusion kinetics | Fick's 2nd Law | Diffusion coefficient $D_p$ | Migration exceeding SML |
| **Nutritional Labelling** | Atwater system | $E = 4P + 4C + 9F$ | % RDA declaration | Incorrect serving size |
| **CAP** | Active gas control | $R_{O_2}$ balance | Setpoint gas ratios | Sensor drift / system leak |
| **MAP** | Passive equilibrium | Henry's Law | Initial gas flush | Package collapse (CO₂ dissolves) |
| **Shrink Packaging** | Polymer orientation | $SR = \frac{L_i - L_f}{L_i} \times 100$ | Shrink temperature | Tunnel temperature too low = no shrink |
| **Cling Packaging** | Tack adhesion | Surface energy match | Cling force (g/cm) | Loss of cling in cold storage |
| **Vacuum Packaging** | Evacuation kinetics | $p \cdot V = nRT$ | Residual O₂ level | Seal failure under vacuum |
| **Gas Packaging** | Gas blending | $V_{gas}$ calculation | Gas purity (≥99.9%) | Wrong CO₂/O₂ ratio |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Package-Product Interaction Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1[Food Commodity] --> nodeA[Sorption Uptake]
    nodeA --> node2[Packaging Material]
    node2 --> node3[Outer Environment]
    node3 --> node4[Permeation Inward]
    node2 --> node5[Migration Outward]
    node5 --> node1
    node2 --> node6[Permeation Outward]
    node6 --> node3
    node2 --> node7[Quality Change]
    node1 --> node7
    node7 --> node8[Shelf Life Endpoint]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: MAP vs CAP Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Food Product Selection] --> check1{Storage Duration}
    check1 -->|Weeks to Months| mapRoute[MAP Modified Atmosphere]
    check1 -->|Months to Year| capRoute[CAP Controlled Atmosphere]
    mapRoute --> m1[Gas Flush at Packing]
    m1 --> m2[Hermetic Seal]
    m2 --> m3[Passive Equilibrium with Product]
    capRoute --> c1[Initial Gas Flush]
    c1 --> c2[Hermetic Seal with Sensor Port]
    c2 --> c3[Continuous Gas Analysis]
    c3 --> c4{Deviation from Setpoint}
    c4 -->|Yes| c5[Active Gas Adjustment]
    c5 --> c3
    c4 -->|No| c3
    m3 --> end1[Distribution]
    c3 --> end1
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Shrink Packaging Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    p1[Product on Tray] --> p2[Place Over POF Film]
    p2 --> p3[Heat Tunnel Entry at 120-180 C]
    p3 --> p4[Polymer Chains Relax and Shrink]
    p4 --> p5[Tight Conformity Around Product]
    p5 --> p6[Cooling Zone]
    p6 --> p7[Sealed Shrink Package]
    p7 --> p8[Ready for Distribution]
```


## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**
Define the term *Migration* in the context of food packaging. List any four factors that affect the rate of migration of packaging components into food.

**Model Answer:**
Migration is the transfer of substances (monomers, oligomers, plasticizers, stabilizers, residual solvents) from the packaging material into the food product, occurring due to concentration gradient-driven diffusion at the package-food interface.

**Four factors affecting migration:**
1. **Temperature** – Higher storage temperature increases diffusion coefficient (Arrhenius relation).
2. **Nature of food** – Fatty foods (high lipid content) extract lipophilic migrants faster.
3. **Contact time** – Longer storage duration increases cumulative migration.
4. **Molecular weight of migrant** – Lower molecular weight migrants diffuse faster.
5. *Bonus:* Film thickness (thinner = faster migration per unit area), polymer crystallinity (amorphous regions permit more migration).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
What is the difference between CAP and MAP? Give one example of a food product commonly packaged by each method.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | CAP | MAP |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Gas composition is actively maintained by sensors and gas injection throughout storage | Gas composition is set initially and changes passively over time |
| Control | Active, real-time | Passive, one-time |
| Cost | High (instrumentation) | Low |
| Example | Apples, pears stored for 6–12 months in cold rooms with controlled O₂/CO₂ | Pre-packaged fresh red meat trays with 70% O₂ + 20% CO₂ + 10% N₂; fresh-cut salad bags |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU Internal Choice Pattern

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper as per 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the three modes of package-product interaction with suitable examples and equations. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the salient features of FSSAI 2020 regulations regarding nutritional labelling on pre-packaged foods in India. What is the Atwater system and how is energy value calculated? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Three Modes of Package-Product Interaction:**

**1. Migration** – Movement of packaging constituents INTO food.

*Example:* Plasticizer (e.g., phthalates) migrating from PVC cling film into cheese.

*Equation (Fick's Second Law):*

$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D_p \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}$$

*Regulatory control:* Overall Migration Limit (OML) = 10 mg/dm² under EU 10/2011.

**2. Permeation** – Movement of gases/vapors THROUGH the packaging wall.

*Example:* Oxygen permeating through a PE pouch and causing rancidity in fried snacks.

*Equation:*

$$Q = \frac{P \cdot A \cdot \Delta p}{L}$$

*Key fact:* Permeation is a three-step process: **sorption → diffusion → desorption**.

**3. Sorption** – Uptake of food components (flavors, oils) BY the package.

*Example:* Loss of d-limonene (citrus flavor) from orange juice into LDPE carton.

*Mechanism:* Partition equilibrium described by Henry's or Flory-Huggins isotherms.

*Consequence:* Loss of flavor intensity in product and swelling/softening of package.

---

**(b) FSSAI 2020 Nutritional Labelling:**

**Mandatory Declarations (per 100 g or per serving):**
1. Energy (kcal)
2. Protein (g)
3. Carbohydrate, with Total Sugars sub-divided into Added Sugars
4. Total Fat, with Saturated Fat and Trans Fat
5. Sodium (mg)
6. *Front-of-Pack* nutrient warning for "High in" salt/sugar/fat when thresholds exceeded

**Other Mandatory Information on Package:**
- List of ingredients (in descending order of weight)
- Allergen declaration (8 major allergens)
- Veg/Non-veg symbol (green/brown circle)
- FSSAI logo and license number
- Best before / Date of manufacture
- Net weight, MRP, manufacturer address
- Country of origin
- Customer care details

**Atwater System:**
The Atwater system assigns fixed caloric equivalents to macronutrients based on their average physiological fuel value:

$$E_{kcal} = 4 \times P + 4 \times C + 9 \times F$$

Where P = protein (g), C = carbohydrate (g), F = fat (g), all per 100 g of food.

**Example Calculation:** For a food containing 10 g protein, 30 g carbohydrate, and 5 g fat per 100 g:

$$E = (4 \times 10) + (4 \times 30) + (9 \times 5) = 40 + 120 + 45 = 205 \text{ kcal}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> Students commonly confuse **energy value (kcal)** with **energy value (kJ)** — for international labels, conversion is: **1 kcal = 4.184 kJ**. Also, dietary fibre (8 kcal/g) is sometimes wrongly calculated as 4 kcal/g. Always read the question carefully!

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Alternative Choice)**

**(a)** Describe the principles, gases used, and applications of **Modified Atmosphere Packaging (MAP)**. Discuss the role of CO₂, O₂, and N₂ with appropriate examples. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the principle, materials, and equipment used in **shrink packaging**. Compare it with **cling packaging** in tabular form. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) MAP Principles and Gas Roles:**

**Principle:** Replace the natural air (78% N₂, 21% O₂, 0.04% CO₂) inside a package with a tailored gas mixture that suppresses the dominant spoilage mechanism while maintaining product quality. The modified atmosphere then changes passively through respiration (for fresh produce) and gas permeation through the film.

**Role of Each Gas:**

**1. Oxygen (O₂):**
- **Beneficial role:** Maintains bright red color of fresh red meat (forms oxymyoglobin); supports aerobic respiration in fresh produce; inhibits anaerobic pathogens like *Clostridium botulinum*.
- **Typical levels:** 0–10% in most MAP applications; 70–80% in high-O₂ red meat MAP.
- **Negative role:** Promotes oxidative rancidity in fats and aerobic microbial growth.

**2. Carbon Dioxide (CO₂):**
- **Beneficial role:** Dissolves into the lipid phase of bacterial cell membranes, causing intracellular pH drop and inhibition of enzymes — strongest antimicrobial gas.
- **Effective against:** *Pseudomonas* spp., molds, and many Gram-negative spoilage bacteria.
- **Typical levels:** 20–80% in MAP; higher in fresh meat, lower in produce.
- **Negative role:** Highly soluble in water — can cause package collapse (especially in cold/wet products).

**3. Nitrogen (N₂):**
- **Beneficial role:** Chemically inert filler gas; prevents package collapse (low solubility); displaces O₂; inhibits oxidation.
- **Typical levels:** 60–100%.
- **Negative role:** None significant (inert).

**Applications of MAP:**
- **Red meat trays:** 70% O₂ + 20% CO₂ + 10% N₂ → red color, microbial inhibition.
- **Fresh poultry:** 70% N₂ + 30% CO₂ → suppresses *Salmonella* and *Pseudomonas*.
- **Cheese:** 30% N₂ + 70% CO₂ → mold inhibition.
- **Fresh-cut salads:** Low O₂, high CO₂, balance N₂ → preserves crispness.
- **Bakery:** 100% N₂ or 70% N₂ + 30% CO₂ → prevents mold.
- **Coffee:** N₂ flush in rigid cans → prevents staling and oxidation.

**Critical Design Considerations:**
- Use **gas barrier films** (EVOH, PVDC-coated, metallized films).
- **Headspace volume** typically 1–3 mL per gram of product.
- Account for **CO₂ solubility** at storage temperature (cold storage increases CO₂ absorption).

---

**(b) Shrink Packaging vs Cling Packaging:**

**Shrink Packaging:**

**Principle:** Polymer films are oriented (stretched) during manufacture below their melting point, "freezing in" oriented molecular chains. Upon reheating (in a heat tunnel), the chains relax to their original coiled state, causing the film to shrink and conform tightly to the product.

**Common Materials:**
- **Polyolefin (POF)** – most common, food-grade, FDA approved.
- **Polyvinyl chloride (PVC)** – excellent clarity, but environmental concerns.
- **Polyethylene (PE)** – heavy-duty industrial shrink.
- **Polypropylene (PP)** – high clarity for retail.

**Equipment:**
- **L-sealer** – for small operations.
- **Heat tunnel** – conveyor through heated chamber at 120–180 °C.
- **Shrink wrapping machine** – automatic, high-speed for industrial use.

**Process Steps:**
1. Product placed on tray or unsupported.
2. Film draped over product.
3. Pass through heat tunnel → film shrinks uniformly.
4. Cooling zone sets the final shape.

**Typical Applications:**
- Fresh meat trays (supermarket).
- Software/CD bundling.
- Multi-pack beverages.
- Tamper-evident seals.

**Cling Packaging:**

**Principle:** A soft, flexible film with inherent surface tack adheres to product surfaces through **viscoelastic adhesion** — no heat required.

**Common Materials:**
- **LDPE** (low-density polyethylene).
- **PVC cling film** (now phased out for food due to plasticizer concerns).
- **PE/EVA copolymers** – modern food-grade cling films.

**Mechanism of Cling:** Van der Waals forces between the film and product surface, augmented by the film's electrostatic charge and surface tack.

**Comparative Table:**

| Parameter | Shrink Packaging | Cling Packaging |
|---|---|---|
| Heat required | Yes (120–180 °C) | No |
| Film type | Oriented polyolefin (POF), PVC, PP | LDPE, PE/EVA |
| Mechanism | Thermal recovery of oriented chains | Tack adhesion |
| Tightness of fit | Very tight (40–70% shrink ratio) | Loose cling |
| Barrier properties | Can be high (multi-layer) | Moderate to low |
| Equipment cost | High (heat tunnel) | Low (manual/manual roll) |
| Speed | High (automated) | Slow (manual or semi-auto) |
| Typical use | Bundling, meat trays | Wrapping leftovers, fresh produce |
| Power consumption | High | None |
| Tamper evidence | Excellent (must be cut open) | Poor (peels off easily) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> Many students write that "shrink and cling are the same thing" — this is a **direct 2-mark penalty**. The key distinction is **thermal shrink recovery vs. ambient tack adhesion**. Also, do NOT confuse *cling film* (household food wrap) with *stretch film* (industrial pallet wrap) — they have different mechanical and barrier properties.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

**⚡ Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**

1. **Package-product interaction** has three modes: **Migration, Permeation, Sorption** — remember the order.
2. **Fick's Second Law** governs both migration and diffusion in polymers: $\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D_p \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}$
3. **Permeation** is a **3-step process**: Sorption → Diffusion → Desorption (Solution-Diffusion Mechanism).
4. **Overall Migration Limit (OML)** under EU 10/2011 = **10 mg/dm²**.
5. **Food simulants:** A (water), B (3% acetic acid), C (10% ethanol), D (oil), E (Tenax).
6. **Atwater System:** $E = 4P + 4C + 9F$ kcal/100 g (Protein, Carbohydrate, Fat).
7. **FSSAI 2020** requires declaration of Energy, Protein, CHO, Fat, Saturated Fat, Trans Fat, Sodium per 100 g.
8. **CAP = Active**, **MAP = Passive** gas composition management.
9. **CO₂** is the workhorse antimicrobial gas in MAP (20–80%); dissolves in lipid phase.
10. **N₂** is the inert filler gas; prevents package collapse.
11. **O₂** is controlled carefully — high for red meat color, low to prevent rancidity.
12. **Shrink packaging** uses **heat-induced elastic recovery** of oriented polymer chains; 40–70% shrink ratio.
13. **Shrink tunnel temperature** = **120–180 °C** (remember this range).
14. **Cling packaging** uses **tack adhesion** at ambient temperature; no heat.
15. **Vacuum packaging** reduces O₂ to **<1%** (pressure < 50 mbar absolute).
16. **Permeability equation:** $P = D \times S$ — high $D$ and high $S$ both increase $P$.
17. **Temperature effect:** Most degradation rates **double every 10 °C rise (Q₁₀ ≈ 2)**.
18. **CO₂ package collapse** happens when storage temperature drops (CO₂ becomes more soluble).
19. **Permeance** = $P/L$ (used in practical film selection).
20. **Unit vigilance** is the #1 source of calculation errors in KTU exams — always check **cm vs mm, atm vs Pa, day vs s**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBFTT604 - FOOD PACKAGING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Active Packaging; Smart Packaging; Packaging Requirements for Raw and Processed Foods and Selection of Packaging Materials; Factors Affecting the Choice of Packaging Materials; Disposal and Recycle of Packaging Waste; Printing and Labelling; Lamination

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definitions & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Active Packaging

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Active Packaging is a packaging system that actively changes the condition of the packaged food to extend shelf-life, improve sensory properties, or maintain nutritional quality, by deliberately incorporating components that release or absorb substances into/from the food or its surrounding environment.*

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Think of active packaging as a **"tiny assistant living inside the package."** While a regular package is just a passive plastic bag sitting there, active packaging *does* something — it might **soak up extra oxygen** (like a sponge), **release antimicrobial agents** (like a bodyguard spraying sanitizer), or **absorb moisture** (like a mini dehumidifier). It actively intervenes to keep the food fresher for longer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Active packaging **deliberately interacts** with the food or headspace — it is NOT just a barrier.

### Main Types of Active Packaging

| Type | Function | Example Active Agent |
|------|----------|----------------------|
| **Oxygen Scavengers** | Absorb residual O₂ inside package | Iron-based sachets, ascorbic acid |
| **CO₂ Scavengers/Emitters** | Remove or release CO₂ | Calcium hydroxide, ferrous carbonate |
| **Ethylene Absorbers** | Remove ripening hormone in fruits | Potassium permanganate |
| **Moisture Absorbers** | Control humidity (desiccants) | Silica gel, molecular sieves |
| **Antimicrobial Packaging** | Inhibit microbial growth | Silver ions, essential oils, chitosan |
| **Antioxidant Releasers** | Slow lipid oxidation | BHA, BHT, α-tocopherol |
| **Ethanol Emitters** | Release ethanol vapor | Encapsulated ethanol on silica |

---

## 1.2 Smart Packaging (Intelligent Packaging)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Smart Packaging refers to packaging systems that monitor the condition of the packaged food or its surrounding environment and communicate information about the quality, safety, or shelf-life status to the consumer or manufacturer, without directly altering the food composition.*

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
If active packaging is the **"assistant working inside the package,"** then smart packaging is the **"reporter telling you what's going on."** It doesn't change the food — it just **reports, senses, or communicates** information. Imagine a milk carton whose expiration date *changes color* as the milk spoils, or a meat tray that *turns red* when the meat is no longer safe to eat.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** Smart packaging = **INFORMATION provider**; Active packaging = **ACTION taker**.

### Main Categories of Smart Packaging

| Category | Function |
|----------|----------|
| **Time-Temperature Indicators (TTIs)** | Show cumulative thermal abuse |
| **Freshness Indicators** | Detect metabolites of spoilage (e.g., biogenic amines, H₂S) |
| **Integrity Indicators** | Detect package leak/tampering |
| **Radio Frequency Identification (RFID)** | Track & trace across supply chain |
| **QR Codes / NFC Tags** | Consumer information access |
| **pH-sensitive dyes** | Color change with acidity change |

---

## 1.3 Packaging Requirements for Raw & Processed Foods

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Different food categories (raw, minimally processed, processed) impose specific physico-chemical, biological, and mechanical requirements on packaging materials. The selection must align with the food's water activity (a_w), pH, fat content, respiratory behavior, and storage conditions.*

### Intuitive Analogy
Think of food packaging requirements like **choosing the right outfit** for different weather conditions. Raw meat (like a sweaty athlete) needs a breathable, leak-proof outfit. Frozen peas need a thick, insulating parka. Fresh salad needs a transparent, breathable raincoat. The "outfit" (packaging) must match the food's "personality" (physico-chemical nature).

### Raw Foods vs Processed Foods – Requirement Matrix

| Parameter | Raw Foods (Meat, Fish, Fruits, Vegetables) | Processed Foods (Canned, Retort, Bakery) |
|-----------|---------------------------------------------|-------------------------------------------|
| **Primary Need** | Respiration control, MAP, chill storage | Barrier to O₂/moisture, mechanical strength |
| **Oxygen Permeability** | High (to allow respiration for fresh produce) | Extremely low (to prevent oxidation) |
| **Moisture Permeability** | Controlled (avoid dehydration) | Very low (prevent moisture gain/loss) |
| **Temperature Range** | Refrigerated 0–4 °C | Ambient or retort 121 °C |
| **Light Barrier** | Essential for light-sensitive foods | Essential for fats and vitamins |
| **Mechanical Strength** | Moderate | High (especially retort pouches) |

---

## 1.4 Factors Affecting Choice of Packaging Materials

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *The selection of a packaging material for a given food product is governed by a multidimensional decision framework involving the product characteristics, distribution environment, regulatory compliance, economic feasibility, sustainability, and consumer acceptance.*

### The "3-P Framework" for Selection

> [!NOTE]
> **Product + Process + Purpose = Packaging Decision**

The three pillars of packaging material selection are:

1. **Product Characteristics** – a_w, pH, fat content, oxidation sensitivity, microbial load
2. **Processing Conditions** – retort, pasteurization, sterilization, freezing, MAP
3. **Purpose / End Use** – shelf-life expectation, distribution chain, retail display, consumer convenience

---

## 1.5 Disposal and Recycle of Packaging Waste

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Packaging waste disposal refers to the end-of-life management of used packaging materials, encompassing reduction, reuse, recycling, recovery (energy), and disposal in landfills. Recycling is the reprocessing of used packaging materials into new raw materials or products.*

### Conceptual Analogy
Picture a **bottle's life journey**: born in a factory → filled with food → shipped to you → emptied → ideally goes to recycling → reborn as a new bottle. If not recycled, it ends up as landfill (a long "graveyard" sleep) or incinerated (turned into energy but with pollution). The hierarchy is **Reduce → Reuse → Recycle → Recover → Dispose**.

> [!NOTE]
> **EU/India Waste Hierarchy (5R Principle):** Refuse → Rethink → Reduce → Reuse → Recycle → Recover → Dispose

### Common Plastic Resin Identification Codes (RIC)

| Code | Resin | Common Packaging Use |
|------|-------|----------------------|
| **1 - PET** | Polyethylene Terephthalate | Beverage bottles, oil bottles |
| **2 - HDPE** | High-Density Polyethylene | Milk jugs, detergent bottles |
| **3 - PVC** | Polyvinyl Chloride | Cling films, blister packs |
| **4 - LDPE** | Low-Density Polyethylene | Bread bags, squeeze bottles |
| **5 - PP** | Polypropylene | Yogurt cups, bottle caps |
| **6 - PS** | Polystyrene | Foam cups, trays |
| **7 - Other** | Multi-layer, mixed | Tetra Pak, pouches |

---

## 1.6 Printing and Labelling

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Printing on packaging is the process of applying information, branding, and decorative elements to the package surface using methods such as flexography, gravure, offset lithography, digital printing, and screen printing. Labelling is the application of a separate printed label carrying mandatory and voluntary product information onto the package.*

### Intuitive Analogy
Printing is the **"package's voice and face"** — it tells you what's inside (information), attracts you on the shelf (marketing), and satisfies regulators (compliance). Labelling is like the **"name tag"** — it carries the official information (ingredients, nutrition, FSSAI logo, batch, MRP, expiry).

### Mandatory Labelling Elements (FSSAI 2020 / Codex)

| Element | Description |
|---------|-------------|
| **Product Name** | Common name of food |
| **Ingredients List** | In descending order of weight |
| **Net Weight/Volume** | At time of packing |
| **Date Marking** | Manufacture, Expiry, Best Before |
| **Lot/Batch Number** | Traceability code |
| **FSSAI Logo & License No.** | Regulatory compliance |
| **Nutritional Information** | Per 100 g or 100 ml |
| **Allergen Declaration** | If applicable |
| **Veg/Non-Veg Symbol** | Mandatory in India |
| **Storage Instructions** | Cold chain requirements |
| **Manufacturer Address** | Name & contact |
| **Consumer Care Details** | Phone/email |
| **MRP** | Maximum Retail Price |
| **Country of Origin** | For imported foods |

---

## 1.7 Lamination

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Lamination is the process of bonding two or more layers of packaging materials (films, papers, foils) together using adhesives, extrusion, or thermal bonding to produce a multilayer structure with combined properties superior to those of individual layers.*

### Intuitive Analogy
Imagine a **sandwich** — two slices of bread (outer layers) with a cheese filling (functional layer). A laminated packaging film is exactly this — multiple layers each contributing a unique property (strength, barrier, sealability, printability) creating a structure that no single layer could achieve alone.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** A typical Tetra Pak carton has **6 layers** — polyethylene (PE), paperboard, PE, aluminum foil, PE, PE.

### Types of Lamination

| Type | Process | Application |
|------|---------|-------------|
| **Adhesive Lamination** | Layers joined with adhesive | Snack packets, pharma |
| **Extrusion Lamination** | Molten polymer extrudes between layers | Heavy-duty sacks |
| **Thermal Lamination** | Heat + pressure bonding | Paper overlays |
| **Wet Lamination** | Adhesive in solvent state | Flexible packaging |
| **Dry Lamination** | Adhesive dried then bonded | Premium food pouches |
| **Solventless Lamination** | 100% solids adhesive | Eco-friendly lamination |

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Active Packaging – Theoretical Framework

### 2.1.1 Mechanism of Oxygen Scavengers

The most common oxygen scavenger chemistry uses **iron-based** reactions. The active agent (iron powder) reacts with residual O₂ in the package headspace:

$$\text{Fe} + \frac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow \text{Fe(OH)}_2$$

The ferrous hydroxide may further oxidize:

$$4\text{Fe(OH)}_2 + \text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow 4\text{Fe(OH)}_3$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Mechanism Summary:** Iron powder + moisture + oxygen → Iron hydroxide (rust). This consumes the headspace O₂ down to **< 0.01%** — far lower than passive barrier films can achieve.

### 2.1.2 Antimicrobial Packaging – Modes of Action

Antimicrobial agents incorporated into packaging operate via:

1. **Immobilization** – agent fixed on package surface contacts microbes (e.g., chitosan-coated films)
2. **Migration** – agent diffuses into food (controlled release)
3. **Headspace action** – volatile agents (e.g., ethanol, essential oils) act in vapor phase

### 2.1.3 Active Packaging vs MAP – Critical Distinction

| Feature | MAP (Modified Atmosphere Packaging) | Active Packaging |
|---------|-------------------------------------|------------------|
| **Atmosphere** | Set initially, then passive | Continuously maintained |
| **O₂ Level** | Drift up over time due to permeation | Actively scavenged to < 0.01% |
| **Shelf-life** | Limited by gas ingress | Significantly extended |
| **Scope** | Replaces atmospheric gases | Adds function beyond passive barrier |

---

## 2.2 Smart Packaging – Theoretical Analysis

### 2.2.1 Time-Temperature Indicators (TTIs) – Working Principle

TTIs are based on **chemical, enzymatic, or physical reactions** that proceed at a rate dependent on temperature. Common types:

| Type | Mechanism | Visual Output |
|------|-----------|---------------|
| **Diffusion-based** | Colored wax diffusion through porous strip | Color movement |
| **Enzymatic** | Lipase/esterase hydrolyzes substrate | Color change |
| **Microbial** | Lactic acid bacteria produce acid | pH indicator color |
| **Polymer-based** | Polymer degradation | Mechanical/visual |

### 2.2.2 Freshness Indicators – Theoretical Basis

Spoilage of protein-rich foods (fish, meat) produces **biogenic amines** (histamine, putrescine, cadaverine) through microbial decarboxylation. These amines change the pH of the headspace. pH-sensitive dyes (e.g., **bromocresol green, methyl red**) change color accordingly.

> [!NOTE]
> **Indicator Dye Examples:** Bromothymol blue (yellow → blue), bromocresol purple (yellow → purple), crystal violet (color loss).

---

## 2.3 Packaging Requirements – Theoretical Framework

### 2.3.1 Water Activity (a_w) and Packaging Selection

The water activity (a_w) of food is the most critical parameter governing microbial growth and shelf-life. Packaging selection must consider this parameter:

| a_w Range | Food Type | Microbial Risk | Packaging Need |
|-----------|-----------|----------------|----------------|
| **> 0.95** | Fresh meat, fish, milk | High (bacteria) | High O₂ barrier, MAP, refrigeration |
| **0.85–0.95** | Cheese, bakery | Mold, yeast | Moderate barrier |
| **0.60–0.85** | Dried fruits, jams | Osmophilic yeasts | Moisture barrier |
| **< 0.60** | Spices, biscuits | No growth | High moisture barrier |

### 2.3.2 Permeability – The Central Concept

The permeability of a packaging film is characterized by its **Permeability Coefficient (P)**:

$$P = D \cdot S$$

where:
- $P$ = Permeability coefficient (mol·m / m²·s·Pa or cm³·cm / cm²·s·cmHg)
- $D$ = Diffusion coefficient of penetrant in polymer (m²/s)
- $S$ = Solubility coefficient of penetrant in polymer (mol/m³·Pa)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield:** Permeability = Diffusion × Solubility. Always remember this for any derivation or numerical question.

### 2.3.3 Transmission Rate (TR) Equation

The actual amount of gas/moisture transmitted through a film per unit time per unit area is:

$$TR = \frac{P \cdot A \cdot \Delta p}{L}$$

where:
- $TR$ = Transmission rate (cm³/day or g/day)
- $A$ = Area of film (m²)
- $\Delta p$ = Partial pressure difference across film (Pa)
- $L$ = Film thickness (m)

---

## 2.4 Selection of Packaging Materials – Decision Framework

### 2.4.1 The Decision Hierarchy

```
STEP 1: Identify product characteristics (a_w, pH, fat, oxidation sensitivity)
        ↓
STEP 2: Identify processing conditions (retort, pasteurize, freeze)
        ↓
STEP 3: Identify required shelf-life
        ↓
STEP 4: Map required barrier properties
        ↓
STEP 5: Evaluate mechanical properties (puncture, tear, seal strength)
        ↓
STEP 6: Match with packaging material options
        ↓
STEP 7: Verify regulatory & sustainability compliance
```

### 2.4.2 Material–Barrier Property Mapping

| Material | OTR (cm³·m⁻²·day⁻¹) | WVTR (g·m⁻²·day⁻¹) | Key Use |
|----------|----------------------|---------------------|---------|
| **PET** | 50–100 | 15–20 | Bottles, trays |
| **HDPE** | 1500 | 5–10 | Milk bottles |
| **LDPE** | 8000 | 15–20 | Bread bags |
| **PP** | 200–400 | 8–12 | Microwave containers |
| **PVC** | 150–350 | 30–40 | Cling film |
| **EVOH (high barrier)** | 0.5–5 | 50–100 (wet) | Layer in laminates |
| **Aluminum foil** | ~0 | ~0 | Aseptic cartons, retort |
| **Glass** | 0 | 0 | Premium bottles, jars |
| **Metal (tin-free steel)** | 0 | 0 | Cans |

> [!NOTE]
> **OTR** = Oxygen Transmission Rate, **WVTR** = Water Vapor Transmission Rate

---

## 2.5 Disposal & Recycle – Theoretical Framework

### 2.5.1 The 7R Principle (Waste Hierarchy – KTU Key Point)

The waste management hierarchy (most to least preferred):

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **REFUSE > RETHINK > REDUCE > REUSE > RECYCLE > RECOVER > DISPOSE**

1. **Refuse** – Don't use if not necessary
2. **Rethink** – Redesign packaging (lightweighting)
3. **Reduce** – Use less material (downgauging)
4. **Reuse** – Refill/repurpose
5. **Recycle** – Reprocess into raw material
6. **Recover** – Energy recovery via incineration
7. **Dispose** – Landfill (last resort)

### 2.5.2 Types of Recycling

| Type | Description | Example |
|------|-------------|---------|
| **Mechanical Recycling** | Physical shredding, melting, re-extrusion | PET bottle → PET fiber |
| **Chemical Recycling** | Depolymerization to monomers | PET → BHET → PET |
| **Organic Recycling** | Composting (biodegradable plastics) | PLA, PHA → compost |
| **Energy Recovery** | Incineration with energy capture | Mixed plastic → electricity |

### 2.6.1 Comparison of Printing Methods

| Method | Principle | Substrate | Speed | Cost | Quality |
|--------|-----------|-----------|-------|------|---------|
| **Flexography** | Relief printing with flexible photopolymer plate | Films, foils, paper | High | Low | Moderate |
| **Gravure** | Engraved cylinder (recessed cells) | Films, foils, paper | Very High | High (cylinder cost) | Excellent |
| **Offset Lithography** | Planographic via oil-water repulsion | Paper, board | Medium | Medium | High |
| **Digital** | Direct inkjet/electrophotography | Variable | Low | Low (no plate) | High |
| **Screen Printing** | Ink through mesh stencil | Bottles, containers | Low | Low | Bold graphics |

### 2.6.2 Inks Used in Food Packaging

| Ink Type | Composition | Curing | Application |
|----------|-------------|--------|-------------|
| **Solvent-based** | Pigments in organic solvents | Air/heat dry | General packaging |
| **Water-based** | Pigments in water emulsion | Heat dry | Paper, cardboard |
| **UV-curable** | Photoinitiators + monomers | UV light | Premium labels |
| **EB-curable** | E-beam initiated | Electron beam | High-speed |
| **Bio-based inks** | Vegetable oils, soy | Variable | Eco-friendly |

> [!NOTE]

## 2.7 Lamination – Deep Theoretical Analysis

### 2.7.1 Adhesive Lamination – Bond Strength Equation

The bond strength of an adhesive joint is governed by:

$$\sigma = \frac{F}{A} = \frac{F}{w \cdot L}$$

where:
- $\sigma$ = Bond/peel strength (N/m or N/15mm)
- $F$ = Force required to peel (N)
- $A$ = Width × length of bonded area (m²)

### 2.7.2 Layered Structure of Tetra Pak

Tetra Pak aseptic carton (typical 6 layers):

```
Layer 1: Polyethylene (PE)         → Sealability, moisture barrier
Layer 2: Paperboard                 → Stiffness, printability
Layer 3: Polyethylene (PE)         → Adhesion
Layer 4: Aluminum foil (Al)        → O₂, light barrier
Layer 5: Polyethylene (PE)         → Adhesion
Layer 6: Polyethylene (PE)         → Food contact, sealability
```

### 2.7.3 Extrusion Lamination – Coat Weight Calculation

$$\text{Coat Weight} = \frac{\rho \cdot v \cdot w}{L}$$

where:
- $\rho$ = Density of polymer (g/cm³)
- $v$ = Extruder output velocity (m/s)
- $w$ = Width of die (m)
- $L$ = Line speed (m/s)

---

## 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---------|---------|-------|-------|
| Permeability Coefficient | $P = D \cdot S$ | mol·m/(m²·s·Pa) | Diffusion × Solubility |
| Transmission Rate | $TR = P \cdot A \cdot \Delta p / L$ | cm³/day or g/day | Per unit area |
| Bond Strength | $\sigma = F / (w \cdot L)$ | N/m | Peel strength |
| Water Activity Effect on Microbial Growth | $a_w$ determines Xerophile/Osmophile/Spoilage | Dimensionless | Critical parameter |
| OTR of Laminate | $1/TR_L = \sum (1/TR_i)$ | cm³/m²·day | Series resistance model |
| Shelf-life (O₂ limited) | $t = (C_0 - C_c) \cdot V / (TR \cdot A)$ | days | Oxygen-sensitive foods |
| MVTR for moisture gain | $t = W_c \cdot L / (P \cdot A \cdot (p_s - p_e))$ | days | Critical moisture content |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** The laminate OTR (Oxygen Transmission Rate) calculation uses **series resistance** analogy — same as electrical resistors in series. The reciprocal of total transmission rate equals sum of reciprocals of individual layers. This is a **favourite KTU question.**

$$ \frac{1}{TR_{total}} = \frac{1}{TR_1} + \frac{1}{TR_2} + \frac{1}{TR_3} + \ldots $$

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Permeability through a Multilayer Laminate

### Problem Statement
A laminate consists of three layers with transmission rates TR₁ = 200, TR₂ = 100, TR₃ = 400 cm³/m²·day. Calculate the effective overall transmission rate of the laminate, assuming steady-state diffusion and the layers are in series.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Recognize that in steady-state, the **same flux (J)** passes through each layer:

$$J = J_1 = J_2 = J_3$$

This is analogous to current in series electrical resistors.

**Step 2:** Express flux through each layer in terms of its TR and partial pressure drop:

$$J_i = \frac{TR_i \cdot \Delta p_i}{L_i} \quad \text{(but } L_i \text{ cancels in normalized form)}$$

For unit area and unit thickness interpretation:

$$J_i = TR_i \cdot \Delta p_i$$

**Step 3:** Total pressure drop is the sum of individual drops (Kirchhoff's voltage analogy):

$$\Delta p_{total} = \Delta p_1 + \Delta p_2 + \Delta p_3$$

**Step 4:** Substitute and rearrange:

$$\frac{J}{TR_{total}} = \frac{J}{TR_1} + \frac{J}{TR_2} + \frac{J}{TR_3}$$

Canceling $J$ from both sides:

$$\frac{1}{TR_{total}} = \frac{1}{TR_1} + \frac{1}{TR_2} + \frac{1}{TR_3}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute values:

$$\frac{1}{TR_{total}} = \frac{1}{200} + \frac{1}{100} + \frac{1}{400}$$

$$\frac{1}{TR_{total}} = \frac{2}{400} + \frac{4}{400} + \frac{1}{400} = \frac{7}{400}$$

**Step 6:** Take reciprocal:

$$TR_{total} = \frac{400}{7} \approx 57.14 \; \text{cm}^3/\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{day}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** $TR_{total} \approx 57.14 \; \text{cm}^3/\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{day}$. The barrier of the laminate is dominated by the **weakest layer (highest TR)** — analogous to current flow in series.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Shelf-Life of an Oxygen-Sensitive Food

### Problem Statement
A 250 ml bottle of fruit juice is packaged in a PET bottle with OTR = 100 cm³/m²·day. The bottle surface area in contact with headspace is 80 cm². The initial dissolved O₂ is 8 ppm and the critical (taste-affecting) level is 2 ppm. The juice density is 1.05 g/ml. Calculate the shelf-life in days.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Convert concentration to total mass of oxygen:

$$C_0 = 8 \; \text{ppm} = 8 \; \text{mg/kg}$$

$$C_c = 2 \; \text{ppm} = 2 \; \text{mg/kg}$$

**Step 2:** Calculate mass of juice:

$$m = V \cdot \rho = 250 \times 1.05 = 262.5 \; \text{g} = 0.2625 \; \text{kg}$$

**Step 3:** Calculate allowed oxygen ingress in mg:

$$\Delta m = (C_0 - C_c) \cdot m = (8 - 2) \times 0.2625 = 1.575 \; \text{mg}$$

**Step 4:** Convert bottle area to m²:

$$A = 80 \; \text{cm}^2 = 80 \times 10^{-4} = 0.008 \; \text{m}^2$$

**Step 5:** Convert OTR to mg ingress per day (at standard T & P, 1 cm³ O₂ ≈ 1.43 mg):

$$OTR_{mass} = 100 \times 1.43 = 143 \; \text{mg/m}^2 \cdot \text{day}$$

**Step 6:** Calculate daily oxygen ingress:

$$\text{Daily ingress} = OTR_{mass} \cdot A = 143 \times 0.008 = 1.144 \; \text{mg/day}$$

**Step 7:** Compute shelf-life:

$$t = \frac{\Delta m}{\text{Daily ingress}} = \frac{1.575}{1.144} \approx 1.377 \; \text{days}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** $t \approx 1.38$ **days** — this indicates PET alone is insufficient for long shelf-life; an active oxygen scavenger or multilayer barrier is required.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Required Barrier Property for Target Shelf-Life

### Problem Statement
A powdered milk product (250 g) needs 180 days shelf-life. The product can tolerate maximum 5 mg moisture gain. The package surface area is 200 cm². If the product is stored at 25 °C / 75% RH (outside), determine the maximum permissible WVTR.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** State the relationship:

$$t = \frac{W_c}{WVTR \cdot A} \Rightarrow WVTR = \frac{W_c}{t \cdot A}$$

**Step 2:** Convert area to m²:

$$A = 200 \; \text{cm}^2 = 200 \times 10^{-4} = 0.02 \; \text{m}^2$$

**Step 3:** Substitute values:

$$WVTR = \frac{5 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{g}}{180 \; \text{days} \times 0.02 \; \text{m}^2}$$

**Step 4:** Compute:

$$WVTR = \frac{0.005}{3.6} \approx 1.389 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{g/m}^2 \cdot \text{day}$$

**Step 5:** Convert to g/100 in²·day for industrial reference:

$$1.389 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{g/m}^2 \cdot \text{day} \times (0.000645) = 0.896 \times 10^{-6} \; \text{g/in}^2 \cdot \text{day}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** WVTR must be $\leq 1.39 \times 10^{-3}$ **g/m²·day**. This indicates a **high-barrier laminate** with aluminum foil or metallized layer is mandatory.

---

## 3.8 Worked Numerical: Lamination Bond Strength

### Problem
A 15 mm wide laminate strip is peeled at 250 mm/min and requires 12 N force at steady state. Calculate the peel strength in N/15 mm.

### Solution

$$\sigma = \frac{F}{w} = \frac{12 \; \text{N}}{15 \; \text{mm}} = 0.8 \; \text{N/mm} = 800 \; \text{N/m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry standard for food pouch lamination:** 1.5–4.0 N/15mm. Values below 1 N/15mm indicate poor lamination.

---

## 3.9 Worked Numerical: Extrusion Laminate Coat Weight

### Problem
A PE extruder outputs 80 kg/h through a 1.2 m die running at 60 m/min line speed. Calculate the coat weight in g/m².

### Step 1: Convert mass flow rate:

$$\dot{m} = 80 \; \text{kg/h} = 80{,}000 \; \text{g} / 3600 \; \text{s} = 22.22 \; \text{g/s}$$

**Step 2:** Coat weight = mass flow per unit area per unit time:

$$\text{Coat weight} = \frac{\dot{m}}{w \cdot L} = \frac{22.22}{1.2 \times 60}$$

**Step 3:** Convert units (g/m² at line speed):

$$\text{Coat weight} = \frac{22.22}{72} = 0.309 \; \text{g/m}^2 \cdot \text{(per second of laydown)}$$

$$\text{Per unit width} = \frac{22.22}{1.2} = 18.52 \; \text{g/m·s} \times 60 = 1111 \; \text{g/m} \cdot \text{min}$$

> [!NOTE]
> In industrial practice, coat weight is expressed in **gsm (g/m²)** = mass per unit area. Typical extrusion lamination PE coat: **15–40 gsm**.

$$\text{CW} = \frac{80{,}000 \; \text{g/h}}{1.2 \; \text{m} \times 60 \; \text{m/min} \times 60 \; \text{min/h}} = \frac{80{,}000}{4320} \approx 18.5 \; \text{gsm}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** Coat weight $\approx 18.5$ **gsm** — within standard PE extrusion lamination range.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
