# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Active Packaging. Give two examples.
**[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

Active packaging is a system in which the package, its components, or the headspace environment are deliberately modified to **enhance the shelf-life, safety, sensory properties, or nutritional quality** of the food. Unlike passive packaging, which only acts as a barrier, active packaging **interacts** with the food product.

**Two examples:**
1. **Oxygen scavenger sachets** containing iron powder that absorb residual headspace O₂ to prevent oxidation of fats.
2. **Antimicrobial packaging** with embedded silver ions or chitosan coating that inhibits microbial growth on food surface.

### Question 2: Differentiate between Active and Smart Packaging.
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Active Packaging | Smart Packaging |
|-----------|------------------|-----------------|
| **Function** | Performs an action (absorbs/releases) | Provides information |
| **Food Interaction** | Yes, directly interacts with food | No, only monitors environment |
| **Modifies Atmosphere?** | Yes (e.g., scavenges O₂) | No |
| **Extends Shelf-life** | Yes, significantly | Indirectly (helps proper use) |
| **Examples** | O₂ scavenger sachet, ethanol emitter | Time-temperature indicator (TTI), RFID tag |

---

## 5.2 PART B: 14-Mark Questions (Module 3 Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 3]**

**(a) Explain the different types of active packaging systems in detail. Discuss the mechanism and applications of oxygen scavengers and antimicrobial packaging.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Definition and Classification (2 marks)**

Active packaging systems are classified based on the function they perform:
- Oxygen scavengers
- CO₂ scavengers and emitters
- Ethylene absorbers
- Moisture absorbers
- Antimicrobial systems
- Antioxidant releasers

**2. Oxygen Scavengers (2.5 marks)**

**Mechanism (Iron-based):**

$$\text{Fe} + \frac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{Fe(OH)}_2$$
$$4\text{Fe(OH)}_2 + \text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 4\text{Fe(OH)}_3$$

The iron powder is embedded in a sachet along with moisture and a catalyst (NaCl). When placed in the package headspace, it consumes oxygen, reducing headspace O₂ to **< 0.01%**.

**Applications:**
- Dried foods (milk powder, coffee)
- Bakery products (bread, cakes)
- Cooked cured meats
- Cheese

**3. Antimicrobial Packaging (2.5 marks)**

**Modes:**
- **Immobilized agents** (e.g., chitosan-coated film — amine groups disrupt microbial cell membrane)
- **Migration-controlled release** (essential oils diffuse into food)
- **Headspace vapor phase** (ethanol emitters release EtOH vapor that condenses on food surface)

**Examples of agents:**
- Silver ions (Ag⁺) — broad spectrum
- Chitosan — GRAS natural polymer
- Essential oils (oregano, thymol)
- Nisin (lantibiotic, GRAS)

**Applications:**
- Fresh meat, fish, poultry
- Cheese, bakery
- Ready-to-eat meals

---

**(b) Describe the various types of smart packaging systems. Explain the working principle of time-temperature indicators (TTIs) and freshness indicators with suitable examples.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Definition (1 mark)**

Smart packaging monitors the **condition** of packaged food and communicates information about its quality, freshness, and safety to the consumer without altering the food.

**2. Classification (1 mark)**

- Indicators (TTI, freshness, integrity, pH)
- Sensors (gas, volatile, pathogen)
- Tracking (RFID, NFC, QR)

**3. Time-Temperature Indicators (2.5 marks)**

**Principle:** TTIs exhibit a **temperature-dependent irreversible change** (color, diffusion, mechanical) that correlates with the cumulative thermal history of the product.

**Types:**
- **Diffusion-based (e.g., MonitorMark™):** Colored wax diffuses along a porous strip; the diffusion length increases with time and temperature.
- **Enzymatic (e.g., CheckPoint®):** Lipase hydrolyzes a lipid substrate, producing an acid that changes the pH indicator dye color.
- **Microbial (e.g., Lifelines Fresh-Check®):** Lactic acid bacteria metabolize, producing acid and changing color.

**Application:** Cold-chain monitoring of chilled meat, vaccines, dairy, frozen foods.

**4. Freshness Indicators (2.5 marks)**

**Principle:** Spoilage of protein-rich foods produces **biogenic amines** (histamine, putrescine, cadaverine) via microbial decarboxylation of amino acids. These amines increase headspace pH, detected by pH-sensitive dyes.

**Example:** Bromocresol purple changes from **yellow to purple** as amines accumulate; printed on a label inside the package.

**Application:** Fish, meat, poultry packaging.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3]**

**(a) Discuss the various factors that affect the choice of packaging materials for food products. Give examples of material selection for at least three different food categories.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Introduction (1 mark)**

Selection of packaging material is governed by multiple factors related to the **product, the process, and the purpose**.

**2. Key Factors (4 marks)**

**A. Product Characteristics:**
- **Water activity (a_w):** Determines microbial risk → high a_w foods need high O₂ barrier
- **Fat content:** Susceptible to oxidation → needs light/O₂ barrier
- **pH:** Acidic foods (pH < 4.5) tolerate more materials; high-pH foods need corrosion-resistant containers
- **Oxygen sensitivity:** Determines OTR requirement

**B. Processing Conditions:**
- **Retort sterilization (121 °C):** Needs retort-stable films (PET, PP, aluminum)
- **Hot fill (85–95 °C):** Heat-set PET, PP
- **Aseptic:** Multilayer aseptic cartons
- **Freezing (-18 °C):** LDPE, HDPE remain flexible at low T

**C. Shelf-life Requirements:**
- Short shelf-life (3–7 days) → MAP trays
- Long shelf-life (6–12 months) → Aseptic + multilayer

**D. Distribution & Storage:**
- Cold chain (0–4 °C)
- Ambient distribution
- Vibration, stacking strength

**E. Regulatory & Safety:**
- FSSAI compliance
- EC 1935/2004 for EU export
- Migration limits

**F. Sustainability:**
- Recyclability preference
- Biodegradability for short shelf-life foods
- Carbon footprint

**G. Cost & Marketing:**
- Material cost
- Printing surface for branding
- Consumer convenience (easy open, reseal)

**3. Three Examples (2 marks)**

| Food | Key Factor | Material Selected |
|------|------------|-------------------|
| **Fresh meat (chilled)** | High O₂ MAP, drip control | EVOH/PE top film + EPS tray + absorbent pad |
| **Cooking oil** | Light barrier, no oxidation | UV-protected PET bottle |
| **Milk powder** | Moisture + O₂ barrier | Tinplate can / multi-layer laminate with Al foil |

---

**(b) Explain in detail the principles and methods of lamination in food packaging. Describe the construction of Tetra Pak aseptic carton with a neat diagram and mention the function of each layer.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Definition (1 mark)**

Lamination is the process of bonding two or more layers of materials (films, foils, paper) to combine their individual properties into a multilayer structure with superior performance.

**2. Principles of Lamination (2 marks)**

- **Adhesion theory:** Mechanical interlocking + chemical bonding + surface energy matching
- **Wettability:** Adhesive must wet the substrate (low contact angle)
- **Surface preparation:** Corona treatment increases surface energy of polymer films from ~30 to 50+ dyne/cm

**3. Methods of Lamination (2 marks)**

| Method | Description | Application |
|--------|-------------|-------------|
| **Adhesive (Dry)** | Adhesive applied, dried, layers pressed | Premium pouches |
| **Adhesive (Wet)** | Adhesive in solvent state | Paper-foil lamination |
| **Extrusion** | Molten PE sandwiched between layers | Heavy-duty sacks, pet food |
| **Solventless** | 100% solids adhesive, no VOC | Eco-friendly |
| **Thermal** | Heat + pressure bonding | Paper overlays |
| **Coextrusion** | Multiple polymers co-extruded in one die | Multilayer films |

**4. Tetra Pak Construction (2 marks)**

```
OUTSIDE → FOOD CONTACT
PE (print) → Paperboard → PE → Aluminum Foil → PE → PE (seal)
```

| Layer | Material | Function |
|-------|----------|----------|
| **1** | PE (12 gsm) | Moisture barrier, heat-sealable, printable |
| **2** | Paperboard (250 gsm) | Stiffness, shape, print surface |
| **3** | PE (12 gsm) | Adhesion between paperboard and foil |
| **4** | Aluminum foil (7 µm) | O₂, light, aroma barrier |
| **5** | PE (12 gsm) | Adhesion between foil and inner PE |
| **6** | PE (25 gsm) | Food contact layer, sealability |

> **Total:** Six layers combine to provide **stiffness + barrier + sealability + printability** for shelf-stable aseptic packaging.

---

## 5.3 Additional Practice Question

**(c) Discuss the various methods of printing used in food packaging. Compare flexography and gravure printing.** **(5 marks)** **[KTU Model Question Paper, Module 3]**

**Model Answer Outline:**

**Printing Methods (3 marks):**
1. **Flexography** – Flexible photopolymer relief plate, fast-drying inks, used for films, foils, paper.
2. **Gravure** – Engraved metal cylinder, recessed ink cells, excellent for long runs of high-quality packaging.
3. **Offset Lithography** – Planographic via oil-water repulsion, sharp images on paper/board.
4. **Digital Printing** – No plate, variable data, ideal for short runs and customization.
5. **Screen Printing** – Ink through mesh stencil, used for premium labels and bottles.

**Flexography vs Gravure (2 marks):**

| Parameter | Flexography | Gravure |
|-----------|-------------|---------|
| **Plate/Cylinder** | Flexible polymer plate | Engraved metal cylinder |
| **Ink Viscosity** | Low | Higher |
| **Setup Cost** | Low | High (cylinder engraving) |
| **Run Length** | Short to medium | Long (best for >1 million) |
| **Image Quality** | Moderate | Excellent |
| **Substrates** | Films, foil, paper, board | Films, foil, paper |

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **COMMON MARK-LOSS PITFALLS – KTU VALUATION 2024:**
>
> 1. **Conflating Active and Smart Packaging:** Students often write them as the same. They are **different** — Active = action (extends shelf-life); Smart = information (monitors). Marks deducted when not distinguished clearly.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the 7R Hierarchy in Disposal:** Students list 3R (Reduce, Reuse, Recycle) only. KTU 2024 expects the **expanded 7R hierarchy**: Refuse, Rethink, Reduce, Reuse, Recycle, Recover, Dispose.
>
> 3. **Wrong Lamination Layer Order:** Students draw Tetra Pak with paperboard on the food-contact side — **incorrect**. Paperboard is the structural middle layer; PE is always food contact.
>
> 4. **Confusing a_w values:** Mixing up a_w ranges. Memorize: Fresh meat/fish a_w ≈ 0.98; dried fruits a_w ≈ 0.6; spices a_w ≈ 0.4.
>
> 5. **Printing method-substrate mismatch:** Recommending offset for plastic films — offset does NOT print on non-absorbent films efficiently; flexography/gravure are correct.
>
> 6. **Skipping diagrams:** In 14-mark answers, KTU examiners expect at least **one neat labeled diagram** (e.g., Tetra Pak structure, MAP gas composition, TTI working). Marks reserved for diagrams.
>
> 7. **No numerical units:** Writing WVTR without units (g/m²·day) — full marks not awarded.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION CHECKLIST – MODULE 3**

### Active Packaging
- ☑ Active packaging **interacts** with food/headspace; passive only acts as barrier.
- ☑ Oxygen scavengers use **iron powder + moisture + NaCl catalyst** → Fe(OH)₂/Fe(OH)₃.
- ☑ Antimicrobial agents: **Ag⁺, chitosan, essential oils, ethanol emitters, nisin**.
- ☑ Ethylene absorbers use **potassium permanganate** (oxidizes C₂H₄).
- ☑ Moisture regulators: **silica gel, molecular sieves, salt pads**.

### Smart Packaging
- ☑ Smart packaging = **INFORMATION** (monitors, communicates).
- ☑ TTIs: **diffusion, enzymatic, microbial** types — cumulative thermal history.
- ☑ Freshness indicators: detect **biogenic amines** (histamine, putrescine, cadaverine).
- ☑ RFID, NFC, QR codes for tracking & traceability.

### Packaging Requirements & Material Selection
- ☑ **3-P Framework**: Product + Process + Purpose = Packaging Decision.
- ☑ **a_w ranges**: Fresh (>0.95), IM (0.60–0.95), Dry (<0.60).
- ☑ High-O₂ foods: EVOH, PVDC, aluminum foil.
- ☑ Retort: 121 °C requires PET/PP/Al.
- ☑ Frozen: LDPE, HDPE remain flexible at low T.

### Disposal & Recycle
- ☑ **7R Hierarchy**: Refuse > Rethink > Reduce > Reuse > Recycle > Recover > Dispose.
- ☑ RIC codes: 1 (PET), 2 (HDPE), 3 (PVC), 4 (LDPE), 5 (PP), 6 (PS), 7 (Other).
- ☑ Recycling types: **Mechanical, Chemical, Organic (composting), Energy Recovery**.
- ☑ Multi-layer laminates are **difficult to recycle** — designing mono-material PE/PE alternatives.

### Printing & Labelling
- ☑ Printing methods: **Flexography, Gravure, Offset, Digital, Screen**.
- ☑ Long runs → Gravure; short/medium → Flexography; variable data → Digital.
- ☑ Mandatory labelling (FSSAI 2020): Product name, ingredients, net weight, dates, batch, FSSAI logo, nutrition, allergens, veg/non-veg symbol, MRP, manufacturer address.
- ☑ Inks must comply with **EC 1935/2004** for food contact.

### Lamination
- ☑ Lamination = bonding **2+ layers** to combine properties.
- ☑ Tetra Pak = **6 layers** (PE/Paper/PE/Al/PE/PE).
- ☑ Methods: Adhesive (dry/wet), Extrusion, Solventless, Thermal, Coextrusion.
- ☑ **Permeability of laminate** uses series resistance: 1/TR_total = Σ(1/TR_i).

### Key Formulas (Recall Drill)
- ☑ $P = D \cdot S$ (Permeability = Diffusion × Solubility)
- ☑ $TR = P \cdot A \cdot \Delta p / L$ (Transmission Rate)
- ☑ $\frac{1}{TR_{total}} = \sum \frac{1}{TR_i}$ (Laminate series resistance)
- ☑ $t = W_c \cdot L / (P \cdot A \cdot (p_s - p_e))$ (Shelf-life from MVTR)
- ☑ Bond strength: $\sigma = F / w$ (N/15 mm standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute KTU Tip:** For any **numerical problem**, always state the given values, the formula used, the substitution step, and the **final answer with units**. Partial marks are awarded for correct setup even if arithmetic fails.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Biodegradable Packaging Systems; Non-Migratory Biopolymers; Testing Methods for Flexible, Rigid and Semi-Rigid Materials
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBFTT604 - FOOD PACKAGING TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Biodegradable Packaging Systems; Non-Migratory Biopolymers; Testing Methods for Flexible, Rigid and Semi-Rigid Materials # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Biodegradable Packaging Systems ### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus) A **Biodegradable Packaging System** is a packaging construct in which the primary structural polymer (or composite) undergoes complete microbial assimilation into naturally occurring end-products such as **CO₂**, **H₂O**, **CH₄**, biomass, and inorganic compounds, under specific environmental conditions (aerobic composting, anaerobic digestion, soil burial, or marine water), within a defined time-frame stipulated by recognized standards such as **ASTM D6400**, **EN 13432**, or **ISO 17088**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Board Definition (must reproduce verbatim in 2-mark answers):** > *"Biodegradable packaging refers to packaging materials that can be decomposed by the enzymatic action of living microorganisms such as bacteria, fungi, and algae into simple, non-toxic molecules (CO₂, H₂O, biomass) without leaving behind ecologically harmful residues."* ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a wooden ice-cream spoon thrown into a garden. After a few months of rain, microbes, and worms, it crumbles into soft, dark humus that feeds the next plant. A **biodegradable package** behaves exactly the same way in industrial composting — it is "eaten" by microbes (fungi/bacteria) that secrete enzymes like **depolymerases**, **hydrolases**, and **oxidases**, which chop the long polymer chain (C–C, C–O, C–N bonds) into small monomers and ultimately into gases and biomass. > [!NOTE] > **Three conditions MUST be met simultaneously for true biodegradability:** > 1. **Bio-assimilation** — the polymer must be a metabolic substrate for microbes. > 2. **Ecosafety / Ecotoxicity pass** — the breakdown products (CO₂ + biomass) must NOT harm plant germination (≥90% seed emergence vs. control). > 3. **Disintegration** — physical fragmentation must reach <2 mm mesh residue in <84 days (industrial composting). ### Classification of Biodegradable Packaging Polymers | Class | Examples | Origin | Key Biodegradation Trigger | |-------|----------|--------|----------------------------| | **Bio-polyesters** | **PLA**, **PHB**, **PHBV**, **PBS** | Renewable (corn, sugarcane, microbial fermentation) | Hydrolysis + enzymatic esterase attack | | **Starch-based** | Thermoplastic starch (TPS), blends with PLA/PVOH | Plant starch (maize, potato, tapioca) | Amylase / glucosidase enzymes | | **Cellulose-based** | Cellophane, HPMC, CMC, NFC films | Wood pulp / cotton linters | Cellulase enzyme complex | | **Protein-based** | Whey protein, soy protein, gelatin, gluten films | Animal/plant protein waste | Proteases | | **Chitosan-based** | Chitosan films/coatings | Crustacean shell waste | Lysozyme + chitinase | | **Bio-PET / Bio-PE** | Bio-based ethylene → PE | Sugarcane ethanol | **Not truly biodegradable** (only bio-based) | | **Mushroom mycelium** | Mycelium-MDF, Ecovative | Fungal hyphae + agricultural waste | Natural fungal lysis | | **Algae-based** | Agar, alginate films | Seaweed | Marine microbial alginate lyase | > [!WARNING] > **Common KTU misconception trap:** "Bio-based" ≠ "Biodegradable". **Bio-PE** and **Bio-PET** are derived from sugarcane ethanol but degrade over **decades to centuries**, identical to fossil PE/PET. Always verify both attributes separately. ### Quantitative Metrics of Biodegradation $$D_B(\%) = \frac{CO_2^{\text{test}} - CO_2^{\text{blank}}}{CO_2^{\text{theoretical}}} \times 100$$ Where: - $D_B$ = **degree of biodegradation (%)** - $CO_2^{\text{test}}$ = cumulative CO₂ from test polymer compost (mg) - $CO_2^{\text{blank}}$ = cumulative CO₂ from blank compost without polymer (mg) - $CO_2^{\text{theoretical}}$ = theoretical CO₂ if polymer were 100% oxidized (mg) Standard pass threshold: **≥ 90 % mineralization within 180 days** under controlled composting (ISO 14855 / ASTM D5338). --- ## 1.2 Non-Migratory Biopolymers ### Formal Definition A **Non-Migratory Biopolymer** is a packaging material whose constituent macro-molecules exhibit **molecular weights so high** (typically **M_w > 1000 Da**, often >10 000 Da) that their diffusion coefficient through the polymer matrix into the food simulant is **negligibly small** at intended use conditions, thereby precluding measurable migration under **Regulation (EU) No 10/2011** and **FDA 21 CFR 174–186** frameworks. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The **1000 Da** molecular-weight cut-off is the EU regulatory threshold. Below it, the substance is treated as a low-molecular-mass migrant requiring an explicit Specific Migration Limit (SML). Above it, the polymer is exempted from the conventional migration testing protocol, provided a **Supporting Evidence Letter** and **documentation of non-digestibility** is submitted. ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Picture a long, tangled rope (the polymer chain) vs. a single loose bead (a small monomer). The bead can roll across a wooden floor (the polymer matrix) easily — it **migrates**. The rope cannot move without breaking; it stays locked in place. **Non-migratory biopolymers** behave like the rope: their giant macromolecules are physically entangled and chemically too large to diffuse out of the packaging wall into the food. ### Sources of Migrants in Biopolymers | Migrant Class | Typical Source | Typical M_w | Migration Risk | |---------------|----------------|-------------|----------------| | **Residual monomers** | Unreacted lactic acid, caprolactam | 90–113 Da | High | | **Oligomers** | Cyclic PLA lactide dimers | 144–288 Da | Moderate | | **Plasticisers** | Glycerol, sorbitol, citrate esters | 92–234 Da | High | | **Solvents** | Residual chloroform, ethyl acetate | 119 Da | High | | **Catalysts / Initiators** | Sn(Oct)₂, benzoyl peroxide | 405 Da | Moderate | | **Cross-linkers** | Glutaraldehyde, formaldehyde | 100 Da | High | | **Macromolecular polymer** | PLA, PHB, chitosan, starch | 10⁴–10⁶ Da | **Negligible (exempted)** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Overall Migration Limit (OML)** under EU 10/2011 = **10 mg/dm² of food contact surface**. > **Specific Migration Limit (SML)** = substance-specific toxicological threshold. --- ## 1.3 Testing Methods for Flexible, Rigid, and Semi-Rigid Materials ### Material Classification by Mechanical Flexibility | Class | Definition | Modulus Range | Typical Examples | |-------|------------|---------------|------------------| | **Flexible** | Yieldable under finger pressure; conforms to substrate | **E < 700 MPa** | Films, foils, pouches, sachets, wraps | | **Semi-rigid** | Self-supporting but bends under load | **700 MPa ≤ E ≤ 3 GPa** | Trays, blister packs, clamshells, thin-walled containers | | **Rigid** | Dimensionally stable; resists elastic deformation | **E > 3 GPa** | Bottles, jars, caps, drums, corrugated cases, glass | ### Universal Mechanical Testing Suite (Applied Across All Three Classes) 1. **Tensile Testing** — ASTM D882 (films) / D638 (rigid) / D3039 2. **Burst Strength** — ASTM D3786 (Mullen), ISO 2758 3. **Tear Resistance** — ASTM D1922 (Elmendorf), D1004 (Graves) 4. **Puncture / Impact** — ASTM D1709 (free-falling dart), D3763 (instrumented impact) 5. **Seal Strength / Heat-Seal Integrity** — ASTM F88 6. **Coefficient of Friction (CoF)** — ASTM D1894 7. **Flexural / Bending** — ASTM D790 (rigid plastics) 8. **Compression / Top-load** — ASTM D642, D4169 (transportation) > [!NOTE] > **Why classify by flexibility?** Because the **mode of stress** encountered during filling, sealing, transport, and consumer handling differs. A flexible pouch fails by **seal rupture or film tear**, while a rigid PET bottle fails by **buckling, top-load collapse, or creep**. The test method is therefore tailored to the failure mode. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Mechanism of Biodegradation — Stepwise Logic ### Stage 1 — Abiotic Degradation (Pre-conditioning) - **Hydrolysis:** Water molecules attack ester / amide / glycosidic bonds. - **Oxidation:** UV + O₂ generate free radicals (•OH, •OOH). - **Mechanical / Thermal:** Disrupts crystallinity, increases amorphous fraction accessible to enzymes. ### Stage 2 — Bio-assimilation (Microbial Enzymatic Attack) - Microbes colonize the polymer surface, secreting **extracellular depolymerases**. - These enzymes cleave polymer → oligomers → monomers → enter microbial metabolism (TCA cycle → CO₂ + H₂O). ### Stage 3 — Mineralization - Final conversion of carbon into **CO₂** (aerobic) or **CH₄ + CO₂** (anaerobic). - Net ecological footprint reduction. ### Stage 4 — Ecotoxicity & Disintegration Test - Compost must support ≥ 90 % germination of two plant species (e.g., oat, cress). - < 2 mm residue must pass through a **2 mm sieve**. - Heavy metals: **Zn ≤ 150 mg/kg, Cu ≤ 50 mg/kg, Ni ≤ 25 mg/kg, Cd ≤ 0.5 mg/kg** (EN 13432 limits). --- ## 2.2 Non-Migratory Polymer — Why Migration Is Negligible ### Fickian Diffusion Framework The mass of a migrant transferred through a polymer film into food simulant is governed by **Fick's Second Law**: $$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \cdot \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}$$ Where: - $C$ = concentration of migrant (kg/m³) - $t$ = time (s) - $D$ = **diffusion coefficient** (m²/s) - $x$ = spatial coordinate through the film (m) For **high-M_w macromolecules**, $D$ drops to **10⁻²⁰ – 10⁻²⁸ m²/s**, yielding migration below detection limit (typically < 0.01 mg/kg) at any practical contact time. By contrast, small plasticisers like glycerol have $D \approx 10^{-12}$ m²/s. > [!NOTE] > **The 1000 Da rule of thumb:** Diffusivity in glassy polymers scales approximately as $D \propto M_w^{-\alpha}$ where $\alpha \approx 1.5$–$2.0$. Hence a 1000× increase in molecular weight collapses $D$ by 10⁶–10⁹. --- ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Concept / Test | Governing Equation | SI Units | Standard | |---|----------------|--------------------|----------|----------| | 1 | **Tensile Strength** | $\sigma_{UTS} = \dfrac{F_{max}}{A_0}$ | MPa (N/m²) | ASTM D882 / D638 | | 2 | **Elongation at Break** | $\varepsilon_b = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0} \times 100$ | % | ASTM D882 | | 3 | **Young's Modulus** | $E = \dfrac{\sigma}{\varepsilon}$ | MPa | ASTM D882 | | 4 | **Tear Resistance (Graves)** | $T = \dfrac{F_{avg}}{t}$ | N/mm | ASTM D1004 | | 5 | **Impact (Dart Drop)** | $E_{imp} = m \cdot g \cdot h$ | J | ASTM D1709 | | 6 | **Burst Strength (Mullen)** | $P_{burst} = \dfrac{2 \cdot t \cdot \sigma_{hoop}}{D}$ | kPa | ASTM D3786 | | 7 | **Heat Seal Strength** | $S_{seal} = \dfrac{F_{peel}}{w}$ | N/15 mm | ASTM F88 | | 8 | **WVTR** | $WVTR = \dfrac{\Delta m}{A \cdot t}$ | g/m²·day | ASTM E96 / F1249 | | 9 | **OTR** | $OTR = \dfrac{V_{O_2}}{A \cdot t}$ | cm³/m²·day·atm | ASTM D3985 / F1927 | | 10 | **COF (Static)** | $\mu_s = \dfrac{F_{s}}{N}$ | dimensionless | ASTM D1894 | | 11 | **Top-load (Rigid)** | $P_{cr} = \dfrac{2 \cdot E \cdot t^2}{D \cdot \sqrt{3(1-\nu^2)}}$ | N | ASTM D642 | | 12 | **Biodegradation** | $D_B = \dfrac{CO_2^{test} - CO_2^{blank}}{CO_2^{theory}} \times 100$ | % | ISO 14855 | | 13 | **Migration (Fick)** | $\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \dfrac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}$ | kg/m³, s, m | EU 10/2011 | | 14 | **Permeability** | $P = D \cdot S$ | (cm³·cm)/(cm²·s·Pa) | Henry's law | | 15 | **Migration Mass** | $M_t = \dfrac{2 \cdot C_{P,0}}{L}\sqrt{\dfrac{D \cdot t}{\pi}}$ | mg | Crank's solution | > [!NOTE] > **Crank's short-time solution** ($M_t \leq 0.55\,M_\infty$) is the migration formula most frequently quoted in KTU exam problems. The factor "2" arises because migration is symmetric into both surfaces. --- ## 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility - **Biodegradable PLA trays** for fresh produce cut carbon footprint by **~30 %** vs. PET (Coca-Cola PlantBottle LCA studies). - **Non-migratory PLA** is FDA- and EU-approved for short-term contact (≤ 6 months) at temperatures ≤ **40 °C**. - **WVTR testing** of bio-films guides selection for moisture-sensitive products (snacks, dry powders) vs. high-moisture (meat, cheese). - **Heat-seal integrity testing** of flexible pouches prevents 8–12 % product loss in supply chains (FAO 2022 report). # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Crank's Migration Equation (Fickian Case) ### Assumptions 1. Polymer slab of thickness $L$ with uniform initial migrant concentration $C_{P,0}$. 2. Food contact surface concentration is held at $C_F = 0$ (perfect sink, worst case). 3. Constant diffusion coefficient $D$ (constant temperature). 4. One-dimensional transport, no swelling. ### Setup of Fick's Second Law with Boundary Conditions $$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}, \quad 0 \le x \le L, \quad t \ge 0$$ Boundary conditions: $$C(0,t) = 0, \quad C(L,t) = 0$$ Initial condition: $$C(x,0) = C_{P,0}$$ ### Solution by Separation of Variables The general solution is: $$C(x,t) = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} A_n \sin\!\left(\frac{n\pi x}{L}\right) \exp\!\left(-D \frac{n^2 \pi^2}{L^2} t\right)$$ Applying the initial condition via Fourier sine series: $$C_{P,0} = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} A_n \sin\!\left(\frac{n\pi x}{L}\right)$$ $$A_n = \frac{2}{L} \int_{0}^{L} C_{P,0} \sin\!\left(\frac{n\pi x}{L}\right) dx = \frac{2 C_{P,0}}{n\pi}\bigl[1 - (-1)^n\bigr]$$ Hence $A_n = \dfrac{4 C_{P,0}}{n\pi}$ for $n$ odd, and $A_n = 0$ for $n$ even. ### Total Migration into Food The total mass transferred per unit area into food, $M_t$, is obtained by integrating the flux at $x=0$: $$M_t = \int_0^t -D\left.\frac{\partial C}{\partial x}\right|_{x=0} d\tau$$ Carrying out the integration term-by-term and summing odd $n$: $$M_t = \frac{4 C_{P,0} L}{\pi^2}\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}\frac{1}{(2n+1)^2}\left[1 - \exp\!\left(-D \frac{(2n+1)^2 \pi^2}{L^2}t\right)\right]$$ ### Short-Time Approximation For $M_t \le 0.55\,M_\infty$, only the first term matters ($n = 0$): $$M_t \approx \frac{2 C_{P,0}}{L}\sqrt{\frac{D\,t}{\pi}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering insight:** Short-time migration scales as $\sqrt{t}$, NOT linearly. Halving contact time reduces migration by only **30 %**, not 50 %. This surprises many KTU students in numerical problems. --- ## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Exam-Type) **Question:** A **PLA film** ($L = 200 \,\mu m = 2 \times 10^{-4}\,m$) contains residual **lactic acid monomer** ($M_w = 90\,Da$) at concentration $C_{P,0} = 50\,mg/L$. Compute the migration into food after **10 days** at **25 °C** if $D = 1 \times 10^{-12}\,m^2/s$. ### Step 1 — Convert Time to Seconds $$t = 10 \times 24 \times 3600 = 864\,000\,s$$ ### Step 2 — Evaluate the Short-Time Formula $$M_t = \frac{2 C_{P,0}}{L}\sqrt{\frac{D\,t}{\pi}}$$ $$\sqrt{\frac{D\,t}{\pi}} = \sqrt{\frac{(1\times 10^{-12})(864\,000)}{3.1416}} = \sqrt{2.75\times 10^{-7}} = 5.244\times 10^{-4}\,m$$ ### Step 3 — Compute $M_t$ $$M_t = \frac{2 \times 50\,\text{mg/L}}{2\times 10^{-4}\,m}\times 5.244\times 10^{-4}\,m$$ $$M_t = \frac{100}{2\times 10^{-4}}\times 5.244\times 10^{-4}\,\text{mg/L}$$ $$M_t = 50\,\text{mg/L}\times 5.244\times 10^{-4}\,m = 5.244\times 10^{-3}\,\text{mg/L} = 5.244\,\mu g/L$$ ### Step 4 — Compare with EU OML Food contact area assumption: $A = 6\,dm^2$ per 1 kg food; density of food $\rho = 1\,kg/L$ → contact ratio = $6\,dm^2/L$. Migration per food surface = $5.244\,\mu g/L \div 6\,dm^2/L = 0.874\,\mu g/dm^2$ This is **vastly below the OML of 10 mg/dm² (10 000 μg/dm²)** ⇒ compliant. --- ## 3.3 Tear Resistance — Trouser Specimen Derivation For an Elmendorf tear test, the average tear force per unit thickness is: $$T = \frac{F_{avg}}{t_{sample}}$$ Where the geometry-specific correction factor accounts for the tear path angle $\theta$: $$T = \frac{F_{avg}\cos\theta}{t_{sample}}$$ Typical values: - LDPE film: **70–200 g/25 μm** - PET film: **30–80 g/25 μm** - PLA film: **20–60 g/25 μm** (relatively brittle) --- ## 3.4 Top-Load Buckling of Rigid Containers (Modified Von Mises) For a thin-walled cylindrical bottle under axial compression: $$P_{cr} = \frac{2\,E\,t^2}{D\sqrt{3(1-\nu^2)}}$$ Where: - $E$ = flexural modulus (Pa) - $t$ = wall thickness (m) - $D$ = mean diameter (m) - $\nu$ = Poisson's ratio (≈ 0.33 for PLA, 0.40 for PET, 0.46 for HDPE) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner Trap:** Students frequently forget the **√3** factor and lose 2 marks. Always derive from Von Mises yield criterion, not pure Euler buckling. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Architecture of Biodegradable Packaging Life-Cycle Assessment ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Renewable Feedstock
Corn, Sugarcane, Cassava] --> B[Bio-refinery / Fermentation] B --> C[Monomer Synthesis
Lactic Acid, Hydroxybutyrate] C --> D[Polymerization
PLA, PHB, PBS] D --> E[Film/Sheet/Tray Conversion
Extrusion, Thermoforming] E --> F[Food Filling and Sealing] F --> G[Distribution and Retail] G --> H[Consumer Use Phase] H --> I[Discard and Collection] I --> J{End-of-Life Pathway} J --> K[Industrial Composting
58 C, 84 days] J --> L[Anaerobic Digestion
Biogas CH4 plus CO2] J --> M[Soil Burial
Slow Mineralization] J --> N[Marine Degradation
Months to Years] K --> O[CO2 plus H2O plus Biomass] L --> O M --> O N --> O O --> P[Nutrient Loop
Back to Agriculture] P --> A ``` ## 4.2 Migration Mechanism Block Diagram (Non-Migratory Biopolymer) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Polymer_Wall A[Polymer Matrix
PLA Mw greater than 1000 Da] --> B[Entangled Macromolecular Chains] end B --> C{Diffusion Coefficient D} C --> D[High Mw Biopolymer
D equals 10 power -20 m2/s] C --> E[Plasticiser / Oligomer
D equals 10 power -12 m2/s] D --> F[Migration Negligible
Below Detection Limit] E --> G[Migration Measurable
SML applies] F --> H[Food Contact Surface] G --> H H --> I[Regulatory Compliance
OML less than 10 mg per dm2] ``` ## 4.3 Decision Matrix — Test Selection by Material Class ```mermaid flowchart TD Start[Sample Received] --> Class{Classify by Modulus} Class -->|E less than 700 MPa| Flex[Flexible Films] Class -->|700 to 3000 MPa| Semi[Semi-Rigid Trays] Class -->|E greater than 3 GPa| Rig[Rigid Bottles and Caps] Flex --> FlexT[Tensile D882] Flex --> FlexTear[Elmendorf D1922] Flex --> FlexSeal[Heat Seal F88] Flex --> FlexWV[WVTR F1249] Flex --> FlexOT[OTR D3985] Semi --> SemiT[Tensile D638] Semi --> SemiBurst[Burst D3786] Semi --> SemiImp[Drop Dart D1709] Semi --> SemiF[Flexural D790] Rig --> RigT[Top Load D642] Rig --> RigB[Compressive D695] Rig --> RigI[Impact D256 Izod] Rig --> RigC[Creep D2990] FlexT --> Report[Pass / Fail Report] FlexTear --> Report FlexSeal --> Report FlexWV --> Report FlexOT --> Report SemiT --> Report SemiBurst --> Report SemiImp --> Report SemiF --> Report RigT --> Report RigB --> Report RigI --> Report RigC --> Report ``` ## 4.4 Biodegradation Pathway Schematic ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Intact PLA Pellet] --> P2[Surface Hydrolysis
Ester bond scission] P2 --> P3[Lower MW Oligomers
Mn drops 90 percent in 60 days] P3 --> P4[Microbial Colonisation
Pseudomonas, Bacillus, Amycolatopsis] P4 --> P5[Enzymatic Attack
Depolymerase, Esterase] P5 --> P6[Lactic Acid Monomers] P6 --> P7[TCA Cycle Oxidation] P7 --> P8[End Products
CO2 plus H2O plus Biomass] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set B] **Define biodegradable packaging with reference to ASTM D6400 standards.** **Model Answer (expected 2–3 lines):** Biodegradable packaging refers to a polymeric packaging material that undergoes microbial enzymatic degradation into **CO₂, water, and biomass** under composting conditions without producing ecotoxic residues. According to **ASTM D6400**, the material must achieve ≥ **90 % conversion to CO₂ within 180 days** under controlled aerobic composting at **58 ± 2 °C** and pass plant ecotoxicity and heavy-metal tests. --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **What is a non-migratory biopolymer? State the regulatory M_w threshold.** **Model Answer:** A non-migratory biopolymer is a high-molecular-weight polymer whose molecular size prevents diffusion into food simulants at intended use conditions. Under **EU Regulation 10/2011**, the threshold molecular weight for the migration-exemption rule is **≥ 1000 Da**, beyond which the macromolecule is considered to pose negligible migration risk. --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) — Focus on Biodegradable Systems **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 4, Q11]** **(a)** Classify biodegradable packaging polymers with **two examples each**. Discuss **three mechanisms** controlling their biodegradation rate, highlighting the role of **crystallinity** and **M_w**. **(b)** A **starch-PLA (70/30)** tray of thickness **400 μm** is composted. After **60 days**, cumulative CO₂ measured = **2.4 g** from 10 g sample. Blank compost CO₂ = **0.4 g**. Theoretical CO₂ from complete oxidation = **16.3 g** (for 10 g polymer, C-content 45 %). Calculate the **% biodegradation** and comment on **EN 13432** compliance. --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks) | Class | Examples | |-------|----------| | **Bio-polyesters** | PLA (poly-lactic-acid), PHB (poly-hydroxybutyrate) | | **Starch-based** | Thermoplastic starch (TPS), starch/PVOH blend | | **Cellulose-based** | Cellophane, HPMC films | | **Protein-based** | Whey protein isolate, soy protein films | | **Chitosan-based** | Chitosan coatings, chitosan-PVA films | **Three mechanisms controlling rate:** 1. **Hydrolytic chain scission (abiotic):** Water molecules attack ester bonds. Higher amorphous fraction ⇒ faster hydrolysis (because water penetrates amorphous regions 10–100× faster than crystalline lamellae). 2. **Enzymatic depolymerisation:** Microbial enzymes (depolymerase, esterase) bind the polymer, cleave C–O–C bonds via the **surface-erosion model**, releasing oligomers that enter microbial metabolism. 3. **Mineralisation via TCA cycle:** Released monomers are oxidised in the microbial **Krebs / TCA cycle**, yielding **CO₂ + H₂O + biomass**. **Effect of crystallinity:** PLA crystallinity > 40 % ⇒ biodegradation half-life extends from **months to years**, because enzymes cannot access tightly packed lamellae. Hence amorphous PLA degrades faster than crystalline PLA. **Effect of M_w:** Above the **threshold M_w ≈ 50 000 Da**, microbial enzyme action slows because active sites cannot accommodate large chains. Strategies to overcome this include blending with low-M_w plasticisers like glycerol. --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks) Given: - $CO_2^{test} = 2.4\,g$ - $CO_2^{blank} = 0.4\,g$ - $CO_2^{theory} = 16.3\,g$ Applying the biodegradation formula: $$D_B = \frac{2.4 - 0.4}{16.3} \times 100 = \frac{2.0}{16.3}\times 100 = 12.27\,\%$$ **Comment on EN 13432 compliance:** EN 13432 requires $D_B \ge 90\,\%$ within 180 days. The measured value of **12.27 % in 60 days** extrapolates to **~36.8 % in 180 days**, **far below** the 90 % threshold. Therefore, this starch-PLA 70/30 tray **does NOT comply** with EN 13432 under industrial composting. The starch phase may have biodegraded rapidly, but the PLA phase contributed only modest CO₂ due to high crystallinity and slow ester hydrolysis at 58 °C. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** > 1. **Do not** subtract blank from theoretical — you subtract blank from test, then divide by theoretical. Marks deducted for reversed order. > 2. **Do not** forget to convert to **percent**, not fraction. > 3. Always **state EN threshold explicitly** to score the final compliance comment. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Focus on Testing Methods **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 4, Q12]** **(a)** Differentiate between **flexible**, **semi-rigid**, and **rigid** packaging materials. List **four mechanical tests** suitable for each category with the governing ASTM standard. **(b)** A **PP woven sack** of width **300 mm** and gauge length **150 mm** fails in tensile testing at a load of **450 N**. If the film thickness is **120 μm**, compute the (i) tensile strength, (ii) engineering strain at break if elongation at break is **18 %**, and (iii) the Young's modulus if the proportional limit occurs at **1.5 % strain** and a load of **90 N**. --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks) | Property | Flexible | Semi-Rigid | Rigid | |----------|----------|------------|-------| | **Modulus E** | < 700 MPa | 700–3000 MPa | > 3 GPa | | **Geometry** | Films, pouches, wraps | Trays, clamshells, blisters | Bottles, jars, drums | | **Failure mode** | Seal rupture, tear | Buckling, puncture | Top-load collapse, creep | | **Test 1** | Tensile ASTM D882 | Tensile ASTM D638 | Top-load ASTM D642 | | **Test 2** | Elmendorf tear D1922 | Burst D3786 | Compression D695 | | **Test 3** | Heat seal F88 | Dart impact D1709 | Izod impact D256 | | **Test 4** | WVTR F1249 / OTR D3985 | Flexural D790 | Creep D2990 | --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks) Given: - $F_{max} = 450\,N$ - $w = 300\,mm = 0.3\,m$ - $t = 120\,\mu m = 1.2\times 10^{-4}\,m$ - $L_0 = 150\,mm$ - $\varepsilon_b = 18\,\%$ - $F_{prop} = 90\,N$ at $\varepsilon_{prop} = 1.5\,\%$ **Step (i) — Tensile strength:** $$A_0 = w \cdot t = 0.3 \times 1.2 \times 10^{-4} = 3.6 \times 10^{-5}\,m^2$$ $$\sigma_{UTS} = \frac{F_{max}}{A_0} = \frac{450}{3.6\times 10^{-5}} = 1.25 \times 10^{7}\,Pa = 12.5\,MPa$$ **Step (ii) — Strain at break:** $$\varepsilon_b = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0} \times 100 = 18\,\%$$ (This is already given; student should state $\Delta L = 0.18 \times 150 = 27\,mm$ for full credit.) **Step (iii) — Young's modulus:** Stress at proportional limit: $$\sigma_{prop} = \frac{F_{prop}}{A_0} = \frac{90}{3.6\times 10^{-5}} = 2.5 \times 10^{6}\,Pa = 2.5\,MPa$$ $$E = \frac{\sigma_{prop}}{\varepsilon_{prop}} = \frac{2.5\,MPa}{0.015} = 166.7\,MPa$$ > [!WARNING] > **Common KTU Mistakes:** > 1. Mixing units (using cm instead of mm for cross-section area) — **2 marks penalty**. > 2. Forgetting to convert **μm → m** before computing area. > 3. Reporting modulus in **GPa without conversion** — always express in **MPa** as per syllabus convention. --- ## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST - [x] **Biodegradable ≠ Bio-based.** Always check both attributes. - [x] Three mandatory conditions: bio-assimilation, ecotoxicity pass, disintegration <2 mm in 84 days. - [x] ASTM D6400 / EN 13432 threshold: **≥ 90 % CO₂ in 180 days**. - [x] **PLA**, **PHB**, **PHBV**, **PBS**, **TPS**, **cellophane**, **chitosan** are key bio-packaging polymers. - [x] **1000 Da** is the EU regulatory M_w cut-off for migration exemption. - [x] **EU OML = 10 mg/dm²**; **SML** is substance-specific. - [x] Crank's short-time migration: $M_t = \dfrac{2 C_{P,0}}{L}\sqrt{\dfrac{D t}{\pi}}$ — scales with $\sqrt{t}$. - [x] Flexible E < 700 MPa; Semi-rigid 700–3000 MPa; Rigid > 3 GPa. - [x] ASTM D882 (flexible), D638 (rigid), F88 (seal), D3985 (OTR), F1249 (WVTR), D1922 (tear), D1709 (dart), D642 (top-load). - [x] Top-load buckling: $P_{cr} = \dfrac{2 E t^2}{D\sqrt{3(1-\nu^2)}}$ — include the **√3** Von Mises correction. - [x] Biodegradation degree: $D_B = \dfrac{CO_2^{test}-CO_2^{blank}}{CO_2^{theory}}\times 100$ — subtract blank from test, divide by theoretical. - [x] Crystallinity > 40 % slows PLA biodegradation by 5–10×. - [x] Diffusion of high-M_w polymer ($M_w > 10^4\,Da$): $D < 10^{-20}\,m^2/s$, hence migration is below detection limit. - [x] Enzymes involved: depolymerases, esterases, amylases, cellulases, proteases. - [x] Bio-PE and Bio-PET are **NOT biodegradable**, only bio-based.

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Tests for Paper (Thickness, Bursting Strength, Breaking Length, Stiffness, Tear Resistance, Folding Endurance, Ply Bond Test, Surface Oil Absorption Test, etc.); Plastic Film and Laminates (Thickness, Tensile Strength, Gloss, Haze, Burning Test to Identify Polymer, etc.); Aluminum Foil (Thickness, Pin Holes, etc.); Glass Containers (Visual Defects, Colour, Dimensions, Impact Strength, etc.); Metal Containers (Pressure Test, Product Compatibility, etc.)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Introduction to Quality Control Tests in Food Packaging

In food packaging technology, **Quality Control (QC) testing** is the systematic evaluation of packaging materials against predefined physical, chemical, mechanical, and barrier specifications. Each material category — paper, plastic film/laminate, aluminium foil, glass, and metal — possesses unique performance attributes, and therefore demands a **customized battery of standardized tests**. These tests protect the consumer, ensure regulatory compliance, validate shelf-life claims, and prevent supply-chain failures.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Testing of packaging materials is a high-weightage topic. Questions are typically asked as descriptive 7-mark or 14-mark problems covering both principles and standard test methods (referenced as **IS, ASTM, ISO, or TAPPI standards**).

### 1.2 Section 1A — Tests for Paper & Paperboard

Paper and paperboard are **cellulosic, fibrous, hygroscopic, and anisotropic** materials. Their performance is governed by fibre composition, basis weight, moisture content, machine direction (MD) vs cross direction (CD) fibre orientation, and calendering history. Therefore, paper tests focus on **mechanical strength, surface energy, dimensional stability, and absorbency**.

**1. Thickness (Caliper)**
- **Formal Definition:** Thickness (also called *caliper*) is the perpendicular distance between the two principal faces of a sheet of paper or paperboard, measured under a **specific static pressure of 100 kPa (1 kgf/cm²)** as per **IS 1064 (Part I)** using a micrometer.
- **Unit:** micrometres (µm) or mils (1 mil = 25.4 µm).
- **Intuitive Analogy:** Think of thickness as the *height of a wall of stacked photocopy paper on your desk*. A thicker wall is harder to topple but takes more material. Similarly, in a paper carton, greater thickness offers better cushioning and rigidity but increases cost and bulk.
- **Significance:** Directly affects stiffness, burst strength, dead-fold, and printability.

**2. Bursting Strength (Mullen Test)**
- **Formal Definition:** Bursting strength is the **hydrostatic pressure (in kgf/cm² or kPa)** required to rupture a circular area of paper (30.5 mm diameter) by a rubber diaphragm pushed against the sample with steadily increasing pressure, as per **IS 1064 (Part III) / TAPPI T 810**.
- **Units:** kgf/cm² or kPa.
- **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine blowing into a paper bag until it pops. The pressure at which it bursts is its *bursting strength*. A thicker, longer-fibre paper can withstand greater inflation pressure.
- **Significance:** Critical for sacks, multi-wall bags, and cartons that must resist internal product pressure or stacking loads.

**3. Breaking Length**
- **Formal Definition:** Breaking length is the **calculated length of a strip of paper that, if suspended from one end, would break under its own weight**. It is derived from tensile strength and grammage.
- **Mathematical Relation:** 
- **Unit:** metres (m) or kilometres (km).
- **Intuitive Analogy:** Think of a long rope hanging from the ceiling — *how long can it be before it snaps under its own weight?* That length is the breaking length. A paper with high breaking length is fundamentally strong per unit mass.
- **Significance:** Indicates intrinsic fibre strength independent of basis weight — useful for comparing different paper grades.

**4. Stiffness (Taber or Gurley)**
- **Formal Definition:** Stiffness is the **resistance of paper to bending under its own weight**, measured as the bending moment per unit width required to deflect a sample through a specified angle.
- **Test Methods:** Taber stiffness tester (most common), Gurley stiffness tester.
- **Units:** Taber stiffness unit (gf·cm) or mN·m.
- **Intuitive Analogy:** Compare a thick cardboard box vs a thin letter envelope — *the box resists bending because it is stiff*. Stiffness controls the "snap" and rigidity of folding cartons.
- **Significance:** Determines carton performance on filling lines, crease retention, and stackability.

**5. Tear Resistance (Elmendorf)**
- **Formal Definition:** Tear resistance is the **average force in grams (gf) or millinewtons (mN) required to tear a paper specimen through a fixed distance**, after an initial slit has been made, using a pendulum-type Elmendorf tear tester, as per **IS 1064 (Part II) / ASTM D 1922 / TAPPI T 414**.
- **Intuitive Analogy:** When you rip open a courier envelope, the force you feel is the tear resistance. *A more fibrous, longer-grain paper resists tearing more.*
- **Significance:** Critical for sack kraft, wrapping papers, and bag handles where tear initiation/propagation occurs.

**6. Folding Endurance (Schopper / MIT)**
- **Formal Definition:** The **number of double folds a strip of paper can withstand before breaking**, under a specified tension, as per **IS 1064 (Part V) / TAPPI T 511 (MIT method)**.
- **Unit:** Number of double folds (logarithmic scale).
- **Intuitive Analogy:** Repeatedly folding a piece of paper at the same spot until it cracks — *how many folds did it survive?* That's the folding endurance. It measures the brittleness vs flexibility of the paper.
- **Significance:** Predicts performance of folding cartons, book covers, currency paper.

**7. Ply Bond Strength (Internal Bond Strength)**
- **Formal Definition:** Ply bond strength is the **force required to separate two adjacent plies of a multi-ply paperboard**, measured in the Z-direction (perpendicular to the sheet plane), as per **TAPPI T 541 / Scott Bond Test**.
- **Unit:** J/m² or ft·lb/in².
- **Intuitive Analogy:** Pull apart the layers of corrugated cardboard — *the force to peel them apart is the ply bond strength*. Poor ply bonding causes *delamination* and laminate failure.
- **Significance:** Essential for multi-layer packaging boards used in liquid cartons and frozen food packs.

**8. Surface Oil Absorption (Cobb Test / IGT Pick Test)**
- **Formal Definition:** Surface oil absorption measures the **quantity of oil (or water in Cobb test) absorbed by a paper surface in a specified time**, typically 30 s, expressed in g/m².
- **Test Methods:** Cobb test (water), IGT pick test, and Kit test for sizing degree.
- **Unit:** g/m².
- **Intuitive Analogy:** Place a drop of oil on waxed paper vs blotting paper — *the blotting paper soaks it up greedily; the waxed paper repels it*. Surface oil absorption quantifies this.
- **Significance:** Affects printability, glueability, and resistance to grease penetration in butter wrappers and fast-food boxes.

### 1.3 Section 1B — Tests for Plastic Films & Laminates

Plastic films are **polymeric, non-fibrous, viscoelastic, and isotropic** (after biaxial orientation). Tests focus on **mechanical strength, optical clarity, and polymer identification**.

**1. Thickness (of Film)**
- Measured using a **dead-weight dial micrometer** with a circular foot of **16 mm diameter** under a pressure of **50 kPa**, as per **ASTM D 374 / IS 2508**. For very thin films (<25 µm), a **gauge block** is used to avoid anvil errors.
- **Unit:** µm or gauge (1 gauge = 0.01 mil = 0.254 µm).

**2. Tensile Strength (Ultimate Tensile Strength, UTS)**
- **Formal Definition:** The **maximum tensile stress that a film specimen can withstand before breaking**, as per **ASTM D 882 / IS 7016 (Part V)**.
- **Parameters derived:** Tensile strength at yield, tensile strength at break, **% elongation at break**, and **Young's modulus**.
- **Unit:** MPa or kgf/cm².
- **Intuitive Analogy:** Stretch a piece of cling film vs a piece of garbage bag — *the garbage bag resists tearing better, indicating higher tensile strength.*
- **Significance:** Determines suitability for FFS (form-fill-seal) operations, pouch forming, and lamination.

**3. Gloss**
- **Formal Definition:** Gloss is the **specular reflectance of a surface, measured at a defined angle of incidence (commonly 60°)** as per **ASTM D 2457 / IS 1064 (Part IV)**.
- **Unit:** Gloss units (GU), 0–100 scale, with 100 = perfect mirror.
- **Intuitive Analogy:** Compare a glossy magazine cover to a newspaper — *the magazine reflects your face; the newspaper is matte*. That mirror-like reflection is gloss.
- **Significance:** Drives consumer perception of product quality, especially in confectionery and personal care packaging.

**4. Haze**
- **Formal Definition:** Haze is the **percentage of transmitted light that deviates from the incident beam by more than 2.5° on forward scatter**, as per **ASTM D 1003**.
- **Unit:** % haze (0 = perfectly clear, 100 = totally diffusing).
- **Intuitive Analogy:** Hold a sheet of BOPP film over text — *if the text is sharply visible, the film has low haze; if it appears cloudy, the haze is high*.
- **Significance:** Critical for transparent windows in pouches and clarity of PET/PP bottles.

**5. Burning Test (Polymer Identification)**
- **Formal Definition:** A **qualitative identification technique** where a small strip of plastic is exposed to a flame, and its **flame colour, melting behaviour, soot formation, self-extinguishing tendency, and characteristic odour** are observed.
- **Diagnostic Features:** *Polyethylene* — blue flame, drips, paraffin odour. *Polypropylene* — blue flame with yellow tip, drips, paraffin/acrid odour. *PVC* — yellow/orange flame with green tinge, self-extinguishing, pungent HCl odour. *PET* — yellow flame, soot, sweet odour. *PS* — yellow-orange flame, heavy soot, styrene odour. *Nylon* — blue flame, no drip, celery/wool odour.

> [!NOTE]
> The burning test is a *first-pass screening test* — it narrows down the polymer family but is supplemented by **FTIR, DSC, or density tests** for confirmation.

### 1.4 Section 1C — Tests for Aluminium Foil

Aluminium foil is a **metallic, ductile, dead-fold, and pinhole-sensitive** packaging material. Tests focus on **thickness uniformity, pinhole density, and barrier integrity**.

**1. Thickness of Aluminium Foil**
- Measured by a **precision dial micrometer with a 5 mm diameter flat anvil and 6.35 mm diameter flat spindle** under **0.5 N force** to avoid deformation, as per **ASTM B 479 / IS 737**. For ultra-thin foils (<10 µm), a **gravimetric method** is used: thickness = mass / (area × density), with density of aluminium = **2.71 g/cm³**.
- **Unit:** µm.

**2. Pin Holes**
- **Formal Definition:** Pin holes are **microscopic perforations in the aluminium foil** that allow gases, moisture, and light to pass through, compromising barrier properties.
- **Test Method:** **Pin-hole Detection Test** as per **ASTM B 479 / IS 15392** — a sample is placed over a fluorescent light table, and pinholes are counted visually or with a photoelectric scanner. Acceptable limits depend on the foil thickness:
  - **9 µm foil:** ≤ 10 pinholes/m²
  - **12 µm foil:** ≤ 5 pinholes/m²
  - **25 µm foil:** ≤ 1 pinhole/m²
- **Intuitive Analogy:** A mosquito net is meant to keep out mosquitoes but its weave has tiny gaps — *aluminium foil pinholes are similar microscopic gaps in a barrier wall*.

### 1.5 Section 1D — Tests for Glass Containers

Glass is a **brittle, transparent, inert, and dimensionally stable** material. Tests focus on **mechanical strength, optical quality, dimensional accuracy, and visual aesthetics**.

**1. Visual Defects**
- Inspection for **seeds (small bubbles), blisters, stones (crystalline inclusions), cords (streaks), checks (fine cracks), splits, and chipped finish**, as per **IS 1463 / ASTM C 148**.
- **Method:** Visual inspection under standard illumination (Diffused Daylight Illuminator).

**2. Colour**
- Measured by comparing the sample with a **Lovibond Tintometer disc** or by **spectrophotometric CIE L\*a\*b\* coordinates** for amber and green glass, as per **ASTM C 147 / IS 1463**.
- **Intuitive Analogy:** A beer bottle's *amber* colour is not a decoration — *it filters UV light and protects the beer from skunking*. Colour specification ensures consistent UV/light protection.

**3. Dimensions**
- Critical parameters: **Height (H), Diameter (D), Wall thickness, Finish dimensions (T, E dimensions)**, as per **IS 1463 / ASTM C 147**.
- **Equipment:** Vernier caliper, height gauge, optical comparator.
- **Significance:** Dimension accuracy is vital for sealing integrity on high-speed filling lines.

**4. Impact Strength**
- **Formal Definition:** Resistance of a glass container to **sudden mechanical shock**, measured by a pendulum impact tester or a drop tester, as per **IS 1463 / ASTM C 368**.
- **Method:** A pendulum of standard mass strikes the bottle, or the bottle is dropped from a defined height onto a hard surface.
- **Intuitive Analogy:** Think of a glass jar surviving a fall from a kitchen shelf — *the impact strength is what allows some jars to survive while others shatter*.

### 1.6 Section 1E — Tests for Metal Containers (Tinplate Cans)

Metal cans (tinplate, TFS, aluminium) are **rigid, opaque, and pressure-resistant**. Tests focus on **mechanical integrity, internal pressure resistance, and chemical compatibility with product**.

**1. Pressure Test (Burst/Seam Strength)**
- **Formal Definition:** The ability of a closed, empty can to withstand **internal hydrostatic or pneumatic pressure** without rupture or seam failure, as per **ASTM B 537 / IS 6306**.
- **Equipment:** Pressure testing rig with a needle inserted through a can end; pressure is increased until the can bursts or the seam leaks. Typical failure pressure ≥ **700 kPa (7 bar)** for food cans.
- **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine stepping on an empty aluminium can — *the pressure test simulates internal pressure from vacuum or pressurised food products.*

**2. Product Compatibility (Migration / Shelf-life Test)**
- **Formal Definition:** A study to ensure that **no harmful interaction occurs** between the food product and the container's internal surface (tin coating, lacquer, epoxy, phenolic resin) under **simulated and real storage conditions**.
- **Tests:** Migration of **tin, lead, iron, chromium, BPA** from the can to the food; corrosion weight loss; sensory evaluation; and microbiological assay, as per **IS 14443 / EU Regulation 1935/2004 / 21 CFR 175.300 (US FDA)**.
- **Intuitive Analogy:** Storing lemon juice in a steel cup will *cause a metallic taste within hours* — product compatibility testing prevents such reactions over months of shelf life.

# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Underlying Physical Principles

Each packaging test maps to a specific physical principle:

| Material Class | Dominant Property | Governing Principle | Failure Mode |
|---|---|---|---|
| Paper | Fibre-matrix interaction | Viscoelastic stress-strain | Tear, burst, delamination |
| Plastic film | Viscoelastic polymer flow | Tensile stress + % elongation | Necking, pinhole, yield |
| Aluminium foil | Metal ductility | Plastic deformation | Pinhole formation, cracking |
| Glass | Brittle fracture mechanics | Griffith's crack theory | Catastrophic fracture |
| Metal can | Plastic deformation + seam strength | Pressure vessel mechanics | Seam slippage, dome reversal |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Insight:** Questions often link the *failure mode* to the *test outcome*. For example, *“Explain why bursting strength is a more critical parameter than tensile strength for a 50 kg multi-wall paper sack.”* — the answer is that a sack is loaded in **biaxial stress** (internal pressure from contents), not uniaxial tension.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.2.1 Paper & Paperboard Tests

| # | Property | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Thickness (Caliper)** | $t$ = reading on micrometer | µm / mm | Direct measurement under 100 kPa |
| 2 | **Bursting Strength** | $BS = P_{burst}$ | kgf/cm² or kPa | Mullen apparatus |
| 3 | **Bursting Index** | $BI = BS / W$ | kPa·m²/g | Normalized for grammage |
| 4 | **Breaking Length** | $L_B = \dfrac{\sigma_t \times 1000}{W}$ | m | $\sigma_t$ in kPa, W in g/m² |
| 5 | **Tear Resistance** | $T = \dfrac{16 \times F}{N}$ | gf (Elmendorf) | F = scale reading, N = number of plies |
| 6 | **Tear Index** | $TI = T / W$ | mN·m²/g | Normalized |
| 7 | **Tensile Strength** | $\sigma_t = \dfrac{F_{max}}{A_{cs}}$ | MPa | A_cs = cross-section area |
| 8 | **Tensile Index** | $TI_{tens} = \sigma_t / W$ | N·m/g | Normalized |
| 9 | **Stiffness (Taber)** | $S = \dfrac{F \times L \times \theta}{W}$ | gf·cm | Bending moment / width |
| 10 | **Folding Endurance** | N (log scale) | No. of folds | Schopper / MIT tester |
| 11 | **Ply Bond Strength** | $PB = F_{delam} / A$ | J/m² or kPa | Scott Bond method |
| 12 | **Cobb Value** | $C = (M_2 - M_1) / A$ | g/m² | Mass gain per unit area |
| 13 | **Grammage (Basis Weight)** | $W = M / A$ | g/m² | Crucial normalizer |

### 2.2.2 Plastic Film & Laminate Tests

| # | Property | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Thickness** | $t$ direct | µm | ASTM D 374 |
| 2 | **Tensile Strength** | $\sigma_t = F_{max} / (t \times w)$ | MPa | ASTM D 882 |
| 3 | **% Elongation at Break** | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{(L_f - L_0)}{L_0} \times 100$ | % | L₀ = initial gauge, L_f = final |
| 4 | **Young's Modulus** | $E = \sigma / \varepsilon$ (in linear region) | MPa | Slope of stress-strain curve |
| 5 | **Gloss** | $G = \dfrac{I_s}{I_0} \times 100$ | GU | 60° angle, ASTM D 2457 |
| 6 | **Haze** | $H = \dfrac{I_{diff}}{I_{total}} \times 100$ | % | ASTM D 1003 |
| 7 | **Tear Strength (Elmendorf)** | $T_{film} = F_{tear} / t$ | N/µm or gf/µm | For thin films |
| 8 | **WVTR** | $WVTR = \dfrac{\Delta m}{(A \times t)}$ | g/m²/day | Permeability |
| 9 | **OTR** | $OTR = \dfrac{V \times 24}{(A \times t \times \Delta p)}$ | cm³/m²·day·atm | Permeability |

### 2.2.3 Aluminium Foil Tests

| # | Property | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Thickness (gravimetric)** | $t = \dfrac{m}{A \times \rho_{Al}}$ | µm | $\rho_{Al} = 2.71 \text{ g/cm}^3$ |
| 2 | **Pinhole Density** | $N = n / A$ | per m² | Visual/scan |
| 3 | **Tensile Strength** | $\sigma_t = F_{max} / A$ | MPa | ASTM E 345 |
| 4 | **Elongation** | $\varepsilon$ = % | % | ASTM E 345 |

### 2.2.4 Glass Container Tests

| # | Property | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Vertical Load (Hoop Stress)** | $\sigma_h = \dfrac{P \times D}{2t}$ | MPa | Thin-walled pressure vessel |
| 2 | **Axial Stress** | $\sigma_a = \dfrac{P \times D}{4t}$ | MPa | For cylinders |
| 3 | **Impact Energy** | $E = m \times g \times h$ | J | m = mass, h = drop height |
| 4 | **CSD (Coefficient of Std Dev)** | $CSD = \sigma / \bar{x}$ | dimensionless | For dimensional tolerances |

### 2.2.5 Metal Can Tests

| # | Property | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Burst Pressure (seam)** | $P_{burst} = \dfrac{2 \times t \times \sigma_{uts}}{D}$ | kPa | Thin-walled cylinder |
| 2 | **Buckling Pressure (Tinplate)** | $P_{cr} = \dfrac{2.6 \times E \times (t/D)^{2.5}}{(L/D)}$ | MPa | L = can height |
| 3 | **Migration Rate** | $M = \dfrac{C_f \times V_f}{A \times t}$ | mg/dm² | Corrosion test |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Important:** Always quote the **standard reference** (IS/ISO/ASTM/TAPPI) and **units** in the exam. Skipping units can cost 1–2 marks per question.

## 2.3 Engineering Utility & Industrial Application

| Test | Real-World Impact |
|---|---|
| Bursting Strength | Prevents **sack rupture** during palletization; determines stacking height in warehouses |
| Tear Resistance | Critical for **opening-force control** of pouches and easy-tear films |
| Folding Endurance | Predicts **crease whitening and cracking** in boxboard cartons |
| Gloss/Haze | Drives **consumer purchase decision** in retail packaging |
| Pinhole Test | Ensures **shelf-life of oxygen-sensitive** products (coffee, milk powder) |
| Impact Test (Glass) | Prevents **bottle explosion** during carbonated beverage handling |
| Pressure Test (Can) | Prevents **seam failure** in retort-sterilized canned food |

# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Breaking Length from Tensile Strength and Grammage

**Given:** A paper sample has tensile strength $\sigma_t$ (in kPa) and grammage $W$ (in g/m²). Derive the expression for **breaking length** $L_B$.

### Step-by-step solution:

**Step 1:** Consider a paper strip of width $w$ (in m), thickness $t$ (in m), and length $L$ (in m). Its mass $m$ is:
$$
m = L \times w \times t \times \rho_p
$$
where $\rho_p$ is the density of paper (typically 700–800 kg/m³ for kraft).

**Step 2:** The cross-sectional area $A_{cs} = w \times t$. The tensile force at break $F = \sigma_t \times A_{cs}$.

**Step 3:** The breaking length is defined as the length at which the strip's own weight equals the breaking force. So set $F = m \times g$:
$$
\sigma_t \times w \times t = L_B \times w \times t \times \rho_p \times g
$$

**Step 4:** Cancel $w \times t$ on both sides:
$$
\sigma_t = L_B \times \rho_p \times g
$$

**Step 5:** Express in terms of grammage. Grammage $W = m/A = w \times t \times L \times \rho_p / (w \times L) = t \times \rho_p$. So $\rho_p = W / t$.

But a more practical form uses $W$ in g/m² and $\sigma_t$ in kPa. Convert: $1 \text{ kPa} = 1000 \text{ N/m}^2 = 1000 \text{ kg/(m·s}^2)$. We use the engineering unit relationship:
$$
L_B \,(\text{m}) = \frac{\sigma_t \,(\text{kPa}) \times 1000}{9.81 \times W \,(\text{g/m}^2)}
$$

Approximating $g = 9.81$ m/s² and the unit conversion factors gives the standard textbook formula:
$$
L_B = \frac{\sigma_t \times 1000}{W}
$$

**Step 6:** Example — A paper has $\sigma_t = 50$ kPa and $W = 80$ g/m². Then $L_B = 50 \times 1000 / 80 = 625$ m. A 625 m long strip suspended would just break under its own weight.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Bursting Pressure of a Thin-Walled Cylindrical Can

**Given:** A tinplate can has diameter $D = 75$ mm, wall thickness $t = 0.20$ mm, ultimate tensile strength $\sigma_{uts} = 350$ MPa. Find the **theoretical burst pressure**.

### Step-by-step solution:

**Step 1:** For a thin-walled pressure cylinder, the **hoop (circumferential) stress** is the governing stress and equals twice the axial (longitudinal) stress:
$$
\sigma_h = \frac{P \times D}{2t}
$$

**Step 2:** Failure occurs when $\sigma_h$ reaches $\sigma_{uts}$:
$$
\sigma_{uts} = \frac{P_{burst} \times D}{2t}
$$

**Step 3:** Rearranging for $P_{burst}$:
$$
P_{burst} = \frac{2 \times t \times \sigma_{uts}}{D}
$$

**Step 4:** Substitute values (converting mm → m): $t = 0.20 \times 10^{-3}$ m, $D = 75 \times 10^{-3}$ m:
$$
P_{burst} = \frac{2 \times 0.20 \times 10^{-3} \times 350 \times 10^6}{75 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

**Step 5:** Numerator = $2 \times 0.20 \times 10^{-3} \times 350 \times 10^6 = 1.4 \times 10^5$ N/m
Denominator = $75 \times 10^{-3}$ m

**Step 6:** Therefore:
$$
P_{burst} = \frac{1.4 \times 10^5}{75 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.867 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} = 1.867 \text{ MPa} = 18.67 \text{ bar}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The actual failure pressure of a can is governed by the **seam strength** (typically 50–70 % of theoretical), so the practical burst is ~9–12 bar, consistent with industry standards for food cans.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Cobb Value Interpretation

**Given:** A paper sample of area $A = 100$ cm² has dry mass $M_1 = 4.80$ g. After 30 s of water contact, the wet mass is $M_2 = 5.10$ g. Calculate the **Cobb₃₀ value**.

### Step-by-step solution:

**Step 1:** Cobb₃₀ is the mass of water absorbed per m² in 30 s:
$$
C_{30} = \frac{M_2 - M_1}{A}
$$

**Step 2:** Convert area to m²: $A = 100 \text{ cm}^2 = 100 \times 10^{-4} = 0.01$ m²

**Step 3:** Substitute:
$$
C_{30} = \frac{5.10 - 4.80}{0.01} = \frac{0.30}{0.01} = 30 \text{ g/m}^2
$$

**Step 4:** Interpretation — Cobb₃₀ = 30 g/m² indicates moderate sizing, suitable for writing/printing paper. For **greaseproof** applications, Cobb₃₀ should be < 20 g/m². For **waterproof** packaging, Cobb₃₀ < 5 g/m² is required.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Test Selection by Material

| Material | Key Tests | Failure Mode Predicted | Typical Acceptable Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| Kraft sack paper | Burst, Tear, Tensile | Sack rupture | BS ≥ 25 kgf/cm², Tear ≥ 100 gf |
| Folding boxboard | Stiffness, Folding, Cobb | Carton collapse, ink set-off | Stiffness ≥ 80 Taber, Cobb₂₅ < 30 |
| Corrugated board | Burst, Ply bond, Edge crush | Box crushing | BS ≥ 10 kgf/cm², ECT ≥ 5 kN/m |
| BOPP film | Tensile, Gloss, Haze | Pouch failure, dull print | σ ≥ 130 MPa, Haze ≤ 2% |
| PET film | Tensile, Gloss, WVTR | Barrier loss | σ ≥ 200 MPa, WVTR ≤ 10 g/m²/day |
| Aluminium 9 µm | Thickness, Pinhole | Barrier breach | Pinhole ≤ 10/m² |
| Glass bottle | Impact, Dimension, Colour | Shatter on line | Impact ≥ 0.4 J, colour ΔE < 2 |
| Tinplate can | Pressure, Compatibility | Seam leak, metal taste | P_burst ≥ 7 bar, Sn < 250 mg/kg |

```mermaid
    S8 --> S9[Bursting Strength kgf/cm2]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] Define bursting strength of paper. Mention the standard test method and the typical instrument used.**

> **Model Answer:**
> *Bursting strength* is the **hydrostatic pressure required to rupture a paper sample** when a circular area (30.5 mm dia) is subjected to steadily increasing pressure by a rubber diaphragm. It is measured using the **Mullen bursting strength tester** as per **IS 1064 (Part III) / TAPPI T 810**. The unit is **kgf/cm² or kPa**. Bursting strength is a critical parameter for multi-wall paper sacks and corrugated shipping containers.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2023] List any four characteristics observed in the burning test that help identify a polymer film.**

> **Model Answer:**
> The four primary diagnostic observations in the burning test are:
> 1. **Flame colour** — blue, yellow, yellow-orange, or with green tinge.
> 2. **Melting/dripping behaviour** — drips (PE, PP), softens (PVC), chars (PET, Nylon).
> 3. **Soot/smoke generation** — heavy black soot (PS, PET) vs clean flame (PE, PP).
> 4. **Self-extinguishing nature** — PVC self-extinguishes; PE and PP continue to burn.
> A fifth useful observation is the **odour** — paraffin (PE/PP), pungent HCl (PVC), sweet (PET), celery (Nylon), styrene (PS).

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Paper & Paperboard Tests

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 | Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Mullen bursting strength test** for paper. Describe the apparatus, procedure, calculation of bursting index, and its industrial significance.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, describe the **Elmendorf tear resistance test** for paper. How is the result expressed? List two factors that influence tear strength.

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Definition:** Bursting strength is the **maximum hydrostatic pressure** a paper disc can withstand before rupturing, expressed in **kgf/cm² (or kPa)**.

**Apparatus — Mullen Tester:**
- A circular clamping ring of **30.5 mm internal diameter**
- A **rubber diaphragm** of ~1.0 mm thickness clamped beneath the sample
- A **hydraulic system** (glycerine-filled) to apply pressure at a controlled rate
- A **pressure gauge** with 0.1 kgf/cm² least count

**Procedure:**
1. Cut a paper sample of size **at least 50 × 50 mm**.
2. Clamp the sample firmly over the rubber diaphragm.
3. Increase hydraulic pressure at a uniform rate (~95 mL/min of glycerine).
4. Record the **pressure at which the paper ruptures** — this is the bursting strength.
5. Repeat for at least **10 samples** in **MD and CD** directions and report the average.

**Bursting Index Calculation:**
$$
BI = \frac{BS}{W}
$$
where $BI$ is bursting index (kPa·m²/g), $BS$ is bursting strength (kPa), and $W$ is grammage (g/m²).

**Industrial Significance:**
- Predicts the **load-bearing capacity** of paper sacks and corrugated shipping cartons.
- Used in **sizing of multi-wall bags** for sugar, flour, fertilizer.
- Required in **QC acceptance** of incoming paper at converting plants.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Principle:** Tear resistance is the **average force required to continue the propagation of an initial tear** through a paper sample, measured by a **pendulum-type Elmendorf tester** as per **ASTM D 1922 / IS 1064 (Part II)**.

**Apparatus (Schematic Description):**
- A **swinging pendulum** with a knife edge and clamping jaws.
- An **initial slit** of 20 mm is cut in the specimen.
- The pendulum is released from a fixed height; it tears the paper and rises to a **follow-through angle**.
- A **scale** (0–100 gf × 16) gives the tear force directly.

**Procedure:**
1. Cut a **76 × 63 mm** paper specimen (or equivalent for 4-ply test).
2. Place in the jaws, ensuring the slit aligns with the pendulum knife.
3. Release the pendulum; it tears through the sample.
4. Read the **scale value in grams-force**; multiply by the **multiplier (16 for 4-ply, 4 for single-ply)**.

**Result Expression:**
$$
T = \frac{\text{Scale Reading} \times \text{Multiplier}}{\text{Number of Plies}}
$$
Unit: **gf (grams-force)** or **mN** (1 gf = 9.81 mN). The result is reported as the **average of 10 readings in MD and CD**.

**Factors Influencing Tear Strength:**
1. **Fibre length** — longer fibres resist tear propagation better (kraft > recycled).
2. **Fibre orientation** — MD tear is typically 1.5–2× higher than CD due to fibre alignment.
3. **Beating/refining degree** — over-refining reduces tear due to fibre shortening.
4. **Moisture content** — higher moisture (15–20%) reduces tear strength as fibres swell and H-bonds weaken.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students forget to report tear strength **in both MD and CD**, and skip the multiplier. Marks are deducted for **missing units (gf vs mN)** and **failing to mention IS 1064 (Part II)**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Plastic & Aluminium Tests

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | 2024 Scheme Pattern]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **tensile strength test for plastic films** as per ASTM D 882. Discuss the parameters reported and the difference between MD and CD tensile behaviour of a biaxially oriented film.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **pinhole test for aluminium foil**. Why is this test critical, and what are the typical acceptable pinhole limits for 9 µm, 12 µm, and 25 µm foils?

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Definition (ASTM D 882):** The tensile strength of a plastic film is the **maximum tensile stress sustained by the specimen during a tension test**, calculated as the ratio of the breaking load to the original cross-sectional area of the test specimen.

**Apparatus — Universal Testing Machine (UTM):**
- Two **grips** (pneumatic or screw-type) with rubber or serrated faces to prevent slippage.
- A **load cell** (typically 100 N or 1 kN capacity).
- An **extensometer** to record elongation.
- Test specimens cut to **dumb-bell or strip shape**; standard width = **10–25 mm**, gauge length = **50–100 mm**.

**Procedure:**
1. Condition the film at **23 °C / 50 % RH** for 40 h.
2. Measure **thickness at 5 points** using a calibrated micrometer.
3. Mount the specimen in the grips; set the **crosshead speed** (typically 500 mm/min for films).
4. Apply uniaxial tensile load; record **force vs elongation** continuously.
5. Repeat for at least **5 specimens in MD** and **5 in CD**.
6. Calculate tensile strength, % elongation, and Young's modulus from the stress-strain curve.

**Parameters Reported:**
- **Tensile strength at yield (σ_y)** and **at break (σ_b)** in MPa.
- **% Elongation at break (ε_b)**.
- **Young's modulus (E)** — slope of initial linear region.
- **Tensile energy to break (TEB)** — area under stress-strain curve.

**MD vs CD Behaviour in Biaxially Oriented Film:**
- In a **balanced biaxially oriented film** (e.g., BOPP, BOPET), the MD and CD tensile strengths are **approximately equal** (anisotropy ratio ~1).
- In a **tensilized film** (e.g., shrink film, cross-linked PE), MD strength is significantly higher than CD due to preferential chain orientation.
- **% elongation at break** typically shows the **opposite trend** — the direction with higher orientation has lower elongation.
- This **anisotropy** is critical in **form-fill-seal (FFS)** operations: film must stretch uniformly to prevent **dog-ears** and **uneven gauge** in pouches.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Definition:** A pinhole is a **microscopic perforation** in the aluminium foil — typically **5–100 µm diameter** — caused by inclusions, surface defects, or rolling imperfections during foil manufacture.

**Test Method (ASTM B 479 / IS 15392):**
1. Cut a foil sample of **specified area** (typically 1 m²).
2. Place it on a **fluorescent light table** (white light source from below).
3. Pinholes appear as **bright spots** against the opaque foil background.
4. **Count the pinholes** visually or with a **photoelectric scanner** (CCD camera + image analysis).
5. Report as **pinholes per square metre**.

**Industrial Significance:**
- Pinholes are the **single largest cause of barrier failure** in foil-based packaging.
- They allow **oxygen, moisture, and light** to enter, dramatically reducing shelf-life of oxygen-sensitive foods (coffee, milk powder, dry fruits).
- Pinholes also cause **leakage** in vacuum-packed and retort-sterilized products.
- Detection is critical before **lamination with PE or PET** to prevent product recalls.

**Acceptable Pinhole Limits (Industry Standards):**

| Foil Thickness | Maximum Pinholes Permitted (per m²) | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **6 µm** | ≤ 30 | Lamination stock only |
| **9 µm** | ≤ 10 | Laminated with PE/PET for snack packs |
| **12 µm** | ≤ 5 | Laminated for pharmaceutical blister |
| **20 µm** | ≤ 2 | Laminated for retort pouches |
| **25 µm** | ≤ 1 | Laminated for aseptic cartons |
| **30–40 µm** | ≤ 0.5 | Semi-rigid containers, lidding |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** In the pinhole question, students often forget to **relate pinhole density to foil thickness** and to **mention the lamination step** that "heals" the pinholes. Marks are lost for not quoting ASTM B 479 or IS 15392. Always connect the test outcome (pinhole count) to the **functional consequence** (barrier loss, shelf-life reduction).

## 5.4 KTU 2024 Examiner's Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Reasons for Marks Deduction in KTU 2024 Exams:**
> 1. **Omitting the standard reference** (e.g., "Bursting strength is measured by Mullen tester" — but failing to write *TAPPI T 810* or *IS 1064 Part III*).
> 2. **Forgetting units** in formulas (e.g., Cobb value should be in g/m², not just "g").
> 3. **Confusing breaking length with tensile index** — breaking length is in metres, tensile index is in N·m/g.
> 4. **Missing the "why"** — always link a test to a **failure mode it predicts** (e.g., tear test → sack opening, impact test → bottle shatter).
> 5. **In diagrams** — sketches without **labelled parts** fetch partial marks only. Always label (sample, load, dial, etc.).
> 6. **In burning test** — students often list *odour* but forget *dripping behaviour* and *flame colour*.
> 7. **For metal cans** — confusing *axial* and *hoop stress*. Hoop stress is always **twice** the axial stress in a thin cylinder.

---

## 5.5 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this checklist for last-minute revision before KTU ESE.**

### Paper & Paperboard
- [ ] Thickness measured at **100 kPa pressure** by micrometer — IS 1064 Part I.
- [ ] Bursting strength by **Mullen apparatus** — IS 1064 Part III.
- [ ] Bursting Index = BS / Grammage.
- [ ] Breaking Length (m) = (σ_t × 1000) / W.
- [ ] Tear resistance by **Elmendorf pendulum** — IS 1064 Part II.
- [ ] Folding endurance by **Schopper/MIT** — log scale.
- [ ] Stiffness by **Taber** (gf·cm).
- [ ] Ply bond by **Scott bond** (J/m²) — Z-direction.
- [ ] Cobb₃₀ = (M₂ - M₁) / A, unit g/m².

### Plastic Film & Laminate
- [ ] Tensile strength per **ASTM D 882** — dumb-bell or strip.
- [ ] % Elongation at break.
- [ ] Young's modulus from initial slope.
- [ ] Gloss at **60°** per **ASTM D 2457** — 0–100 GU.
- [ ] Haze per **ASTM D 1003** — % scattered light.
- [ ] Burning test: flame colour, drip, soot, odour, self-extinguishing.
- [ ] **PE** — blue flame, drips, paraffin smell.
- [ ] **PP** — blue with yellow tip, drips, floats on water.
- [ ] **PVC** — yellow with green, self-extinguishing, HCl smell.
- [ ] **PET** — yellow, soot, sweet smell.
- [ ] **PS** — yellow-orange, heavy soot, styrene smell.

### Aluminium Foil
- [ ] Thickness by micrometer or **gravimetric** (ρ = 2.71 g/cm³).
- [ ] Pinhole test on **fluorescent light table** — ASTM B 479.
- [ ] 9 µm → ≤ 10 pinholes/m².
- [ ] 12 µm → ≤ 5 pinholes/m².
- [ ] 25 µm → ≤ 1 pinhole/m².

### Glass Container
- [ ] Visual defects: seeds, blisters, stones, cords, checks, splits.
- [ ] Colour by Lovibond or **CIE L\*a\*b\*** — amber for UV protection.
- [ ] Dimensions: height, diameter, wall thickness, T & E (finish).
- [ ] Impact test by **pendulum** or **drop** — measured in Joules.
- [ ] Hoop stress = P·D / (2t); Axial stress = P·D / (4t).

### Metal Container
- [ ] Pressure test per **ASTM B 537** — typically ≥ 7 bar for food cans.
- [ ] Burst pressure (theoretical) = 2t·σ_uts / D.
- [ ] Buckling pressure for tinplate = 2.6·E·(t/D)^2.5 / (L/D).
- [ ] Product compatibility: migration of Sn, Pb, Fe, BPA, Cr; per **IS 14443 / EU 1935/2004**.
- [ ] Common tests: corrosion weight loss, sensory, microbiological.

### High-Yield Formula Quick-Recall
- Breaking length: $L_B = \dfrac{\sigma_t \times 1000}{W}$
- Bursting index: $BI = \dfrac{BS}{W}$
- Cobb: $C_{30} = \dfrac{M_2 - M_1}{A}$
- % Elongation: $\varepsilon = \dfrac{L_f - L_0}{L_0} \times 100$
- Hoop stress: $\sigma_h = \dfrac{P \cdot D}{2t}$
- Burst pressure: $P_{burst} = \dfrac{2t \cdot \sigma_{uts}}{D}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Tip:** Always present a **labeled diagram** for any 7-mark question. A well-drawn schematic of the Mullen tester, Elmendorf tester, or can pressure rig will fetch **at least 2–3 easy marks** even if your written answer is incomplete. Practice these diagrams before the exam.
